issue_comment_body: Thanks!

Thanks!

Do you plan to make any other contributions apart from this one and #1 or should I just make a release as soon as possible?

since naz responds to requests from SMSC as soon as it gets them, I do not foresee `naz` getting into situations where it is lagging behind any SMSC that happens to have window size limits. 

if `naz` ever finds itself in such a situation; then it is probably because it has some perfomance bottlenecks(say for example if `naz` is waiting for a network request etc). 
if that were to be the case; we should fix those perfomance bugs

https://github.com/lazka/pgi/pull/25 I've extended the above script to loop through all typelibs it can find.

It has skips at least one module it has trouble importing (ideally I guess it should create a new python instance to try each new import).
(using 3.4.3)

Closing due to inactivity.

Please reopen if your query was unanswered above.

Thanks. It's curious that `flake8` linter did not alert me about this.

It's fixed on `master`, but I'm still working on `v0.1.3` with some major features and general improvements and it would probably take me the rest of the week to make the release.

I'm not sure why that code has disappeared but cherry-picking seems like a good solution.

For code that's easily tested I prefer to go with regular unit tests such as these: https://github.com/WoLpH/mt940/blob/develop/tests/test_processors.py

Beyond that I've tried got gather as much mt940 files as possible to do integration tests which are run here: https://github.com/WoLpH/mt940/blob/de75e5a4826b9a5ee2bcd10ade18317baa298b2f/tests/test_sta_parsing.py#L77-L106

To generate your own yml files from the mta files, temporarily uncomment this line: https://github.com/WoLpH/mt940/blob/de75e5a4826b9a5ee2bcd10ade18317baa298b2f/tests/test_sta_parsing.py#L82

@cdent feel free to close this one if duplicated with another SSL bug. Not sure it was related though.

If you really want it and you're willing to implement it that's fine with but some constraints:

* It should default to off (as you've already said)
* Even if it is turned on, if the output is not a tty then it should be turned off (getting ansi escape sequences in log files is zomg the worst thing ever). [isatty](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/858623/how-to-recognize-whether-a-script-is-running-on-a-tty) ought to do that job.

The dependencies are conditional. The RPi.GPIO module is only required when accessing hardware via GPIO, and the smbus module is only required when accessing hardware via I²C.

This could probably be solved using pip categories(?), e.g. "pip install RPLCD[i2c]", I don't have any experience with those though. @gyscos maybe you?

>  there really isn't a way to detect browser leave event caused by user hitting Ctrl+w rather than closing it by clicking on the tab close button, or closing browsers, right?

That's correct. And it's actually fine, because you probably don't want to close the tab via click too, because you will lose your work. That's how most of terminals work: if you have a process somewhere, they will ask you if you really want to close the terminal.

In web-term I added something like this: `term.on("close", window.close.bind(window))`. That will close the tab when the terminal is closed.

`Shift+w` is obviously not something you want because that simply means uppercase `W`. :joy: 
But that's configurable: create (or edit existing) the `~/.inputrc` file and add this in it:

```
Control-F: backward-kill-word
```

That means <kbd>Ctrl</kbd> + <kbd>F</kbd> will delete the last word. It does work in terminal, but not in VIM. I'm sure it's fixable by adding some shortcut in VIM, tho. :smile:

```python
x = (10 * ureg.feet / 3 * ureg.inches)

y = x * (10 * ureg.inches)
```
If you always reduce, you can invoke unnecessary work, which is non-trivial for large datasets. Having an opt-in flag would be ok. It would also be ok (IMO) if `.magnitude` and calls to `array()` caused dimensionless quantities to auto reduce.

This is by design. What is the use case that requires adding two quantities with temperature unit degC? Often one of the summands is a delta_unit. The only exception that I can imagine is averaging multiple measurements. But in case of averaging you conceptually have an array of numbers of the same unit but not an array of quantities.

You really should learn to use feature branches ;)

My own workflow is as follows, I've found it quite simple and effective:
```
git fetch  # Fetches the current states of the branches
git checkout -b feature_XXY upstream/master  # Checks out a new branch, starting from where upstream/master is
*make changes and commit them*
git push origin feature_XXY  # Pushes the commits to a branch in origin (your fork)
```

This way, the new changes always start from where the upstream (in this case, my repo) is. For this to work, of course you need a remote pointing to "upstream": ```git remote add upstream git@github.com:kipe/enocean.git```

By using this method, if pull request isn't merged, it doesn't affect anything and allows you to work on multiple issues/features at the same time. Also, if for some reason PR #18 isn't merged, it makes it very hard to merge this. If it was referencing the current master, it'd be a lot easier ;)

That being said, I'll try and leave work earlier and get these PRs reviewed and merged. For the future, I'd appreciate using feature branches referring to the current state of upstream though ;)

Dear Jacob,

this does not broke KISSness (it is an optional feature that "advanced" user can exploit, but it is not mandatory to use), and I am actually working on that, not just "consider" it.

What we have to decide is how implement it. My third point is just the main issue. Let me to be more clear. How do you like to track the modifications on libfoo.a? The modifications of libfoo.a will trigger the rebuild of main program, but presently if the libfoo.a is *simply* linked you cannot be advised of any changes of libfoo.a sources... With foraytool you have 2 targets one depending on the other, not just one target with a linked library... 

Now, I can view 2 different approaches (and we can try implement both of them, not just one):

+ create in FoBiS.py a mechanism similar to foraytool to create 2 (or more) targets with one depending on the other; this approach, I think, is simpler with fobos rather than the command line;
+ add another mechanism to track libfoo.a modifications; presently the only (simple) way I see for this approach is to the check a hash (e.g. md5sum) of the libfoo.a: 
    - build your main and concurrently save the hash of libfoo.a aside the library;
    - modify libfoo, thus create a new libfoo.a;
    - FoBiS.py build main_project: now FoBiS.py checks **ALSO** the hash of libfoo.a and recompile the main because the hash differs from the old previously stored.

Do you agree? Any other suggestions are welcome.

See you soon.

According to PDF 1.7 spec, section 3.3. (LZW and Flate Predictor Functions):

http://www.adobe.com/content/dam/Adobe/en/devnet/acrobat/pdfs/pdf_reference_1-7.pdf

> The second supported group of predictor functions, the PNG group, consists of
the filters of the World Wide Web Consortium’s Portable Network Graphics
recommendation, documented in Internet RFC 2083, PNG (Portable Network
Graphics) Specification (see the Bibliography). The term predictors is used here
instead of filters to avoid confusion. There are five basic PNG predictor
algorithms (and a sixth that chooses the optimum predictor function separately
for each row):

| - | - |
| --- | --- |
| None | No prediction |
| Sub | Predicts the same as the sample to the left |
| Up | Predicts the same as the sample above |
| Average | Predicts the average of the sample to the left and the sample above |
| Paeth | A nonlinear function of the sample above, the sample to the left, and the sample to the upper left |

> The predictor algorithm to be used, if any, is indicated by the Predictor filter
parameter (see Table 3.7), which can have any of the values listed in Table 3.8. 

TABLE 3.8 Predictor values

| VALUE | MEANING |
| --- | --- |
| 1 No prediction (the default value) |
|2 | TIFF Predictor 2 |
| 10 | PNG prediction (on encoding, PNG None on all rows) |
| 11  | PNG prediction (on encoding, PNG Sub on all rows) |
| 12 | PNG prediction (on encoding, PNG Up on all rows) |
| 13 | PNG prediction (on encoding, PNG Average on all rows) |
| 14 | PNG prediction (on encoding, PNG Paeth on all rows) |
| 15 | PNG prediction (on encoding, PNG optimum) |

> For LZWDecode and FlateDecode, a Predictor value greater than or equal to 10
merely indicates that a PNG predictor is in use; the specific predictor function
used is explicitly encoded in the incoming data. The value of Predictor supplied
by the decoding filter need not match the value used when the data was encoded
if they are both greater than or equal to 10. 

> The two groups of predictor functions have some commonalities. Both make the
following assumptions:
• Data is presented in order, from the top row to the bottom row and, within a
row, from left to right.
• A row occupies a whole number of bytes, rounded up if necessary.
• Samples and their components are packed into bytes from high-order to loworder
bits.
• All color components of samples outside the image (which are necessary for
predictions near the boundaries) are 0.
The predictor function groups also differ in significant ways:
• The postprediction data for each PNG-predicted row begins with an explicit
algorithm tag; therefore, different rows can be predicted with different algorithms
to improve compression. TIFF Predictor 2 has no such identifier; the
same algorithm applies to all rows.
• The TIFF function group predicts each color component from the prior instance
of that component, taking into account the number of bits per component
and components per sample. In contrast, the PNG function group
predicts each byte of data as a function of the corresponding byte of one or more previous image samples, regardless of whether there are multiple color
components in a byte or whether a single color component spans multiple
bytes. This can yield significantly better speed at the cost of somewhat worse
compression.

Well, I'm not taking the precondition checks out of dlib.  It's one of the
main dlib features that makes debugging easier.  Alternatively, leaving
them on outside debug mode is a huge performance hit so they can't be on
all the time either.  It should also be noted that dlib initially used
assert() to indicate these errors but switched to using exceptions because
of complains from embedded developers working on platforms where it's much
easier to log an exception's message than it is to redirect the output of
assert() to somewhere a human can see it. So exceptions it is.

Finally, the reason there is the second check followed by abort() is to
prevent someone from accidentally writing code that depends on those
exceptions. In fact, that's what happened to @col-b. He had code that tried
to use them for user input validation and got the nasty dlib message saying
he shouldn't do that. If I had removed that second check he would have
instead unknowingly sent his application out, in release mode where the
debugging checks are gone, and users would have been greeted with
segmentation faults or other bad behavior. The only reason this was brought
to his attention is because of the "second check then abort()" feature you
are complaining about.  However, without that bad things would have
happened.

I have written some class definitions, but don't have a solid idea in my head yet.

Python:

```python
class _CurveData(collections.namedtuple(
        '_CurveData',
        ('num_nodes dimension start end root nodes'))):
    """Data describing a B |eacute| zier curve.

    This is a low-level data container, intended to mirror a type that can
    be used from C, Cython, Fortran, etc.

    Here, since ``nodes`` encode shape data, storing both a NumPy array
    **and** its shape (as ``num_nodes`` and ``dimension``) is wasteful.

    However, in other languages, ``nodes`` could simply be a pointer, so
    this information (along with stride information) would be necessary.

    Args:
        num_nodes (int): The number of nodes that define the curve.
        dimension (int): The dimension that the curve lies in, should
            be the number of columns in ``nodes``.
        start (Optional[float]): The beginning of the sub-interval
            that this curve represents.
        end (Optional[float]): The end of the sub-interval
            that this curve represents.
        root (Optional[_CurveData]): The root curve that contains this
            current curve. If :data:`None` it means this curve is its
            own root.
        nodes (numpy.ndarray): The nodes in the curve. The rows
            represent each node while the columns are the dimension
            of the ambient space.
    """

    __slots__ = ()
```

Fortran (some of these fields aren't allowed with `bind(c)`, so this is not valid Fortran code):

```fortran
type, bind(c) :: CurveData
   real(c_double), pointer :: nodes(:, :)
   integer(c_int) :: num_nodes
   integer(c_int) :: dimension_
   real(c_double) :: start
   real(c_double) :: end_
   type(CurveData), pointer :: root
end type CurveData
```

C:

```c
typedef struct Curve {
  double *nodes;
  int num_nodes;
  int dimension;
  double start;
  double end;
  struct Curve *root;
} Curve;
```

One question about input validation (mainly directed to @urinieto ): should tmeasure require that the inputs are strict hierarchies (olda-style) or should we also allow inconsistent multi-level segmentations (lsd-style)?

Currently, there's a check for consistency by verifying that boundaries at layers 1..k-1 are included in layer k.  This is necessary for the derivation of tmeasure, but I'm not sure it's necessary for the application.

The semantics of tmeasure are, basically, "count all frame triples (q, i, j) such that (q,i) are closer than (q, j)", where "closeness" is measured by comparing the deepest segments containing both elements of each pair.  As far as I can tell, this would work fine with inconsistent segmentations, in that it's still possible to build the pairwise depth matrix.  I have no idea if the resulting scores would be all that meaningful, but it seems plausible enough, given that we have implicit temporal continuity from the nature of the input anyway.

So: maybe we should relax the exception here to a warning? (Warnings can be caught and treated as exceptions later on anyway, if that's what the user is into.)

Please advise: I don't have strong opinions either way on this.

So I think what you would want is the client to fire off a completion request, do something else in the meantime (i.e. be responsive to the user), then get the result of the completion request when it's done and then show the result to the user (if it still makes sense and the user isn't on another word by now, right?). All this just means that the client needs to be async (i.e. doing something else while jedi is working). Now in plain python this is easy, regardless of stdin/out, sockets direct connection or HTTP. Question is whether something like vim can do it (I know very little about vim, but I seem to remember that it doesn't like having to keep background processes / other threads alive while offering user interaction). If that's true, that limits the options, and we'd need something like HTTP, where a user presses `os.p`, vim then fires off an HTTP request to jedi asking for completion, closes the connection, and after 100ms does another HTTP request asking jedi if it has an answer already, and possibly another if there is still no answer yet. @davidhalter & @vheon 
Just to be clear, there are 2 choices: connection (stdout / unix (or NT) sockets or TCP port). stdin/out is probably easiest, TCP port will definitely need HMAC, unix sockets probably need HMAC (or one might circumvent security policies on SELinux, I think, possibly ;) )

Second choice is protocol: one connection, simple protocol (only choice for stdin/out). Easy for jedi to find out that it needs to stop since the client died. Or HTTP (-like) multi-connection (sure, can be one connection through keep-alive, but a broken connection doesn't mean anything, and a single client may (probably will) make multiple connections belong to the same person), having the advantage that a client will not have to keep a connection open.

The same operation won't work on all platforms because Windows returns different values than the Unixes do. I don't think a change needs to be made - just updating the documentation should be enough. However, if code _is_ to be adjusted so that it returns milliseconds on Windows, just detect if the OS is Windows and divide by `1e6` instead of `1e3`, without the multiplication by `10` first.

Is there any reason not to just return microseconds on all platforms? The adjustment for Windows would still be needed (division by `1000`) but I think it's better to just return the most precise value available and leave it to the user to adjust it to the order of magnitude they wish. As long as the documentation is accurate, this shouldn't be a problem although `psutil` is widely used enough that changing the order of magnitude abruptly might not be a good idea.

There can be a compromise: return both values separately (raw and milliseconds) and just adjust the value differently on Windows to get the ms.

@bot.event

I started refactoring the script to suit my own stylistic preferences, and in the process, I am making sure the script works on Python versions 2.6+ and 3.x. You actually wouldn't need to make a ton of changes to support 2.6 and 3.x. All of the urllib and urllib2 functionality your script uses also exists in Python 3. At the top of my script, I have this block:

    if sys.version_info < (3, ):
        from urllib2 import HTTPError, urlopen
        from urlparse import urljoin
    else:
        from urllib.error import HTTPError
        from urllib.parse import urljoin
        from urllib.request import urlopen

The argparse module doesn't exist in 2.6, so I replaced it with getopt. To support Python 3, I will also need to A) modify the regular expressions to use bytes instead of strings, or B) make sure all subprocess output is converted to strings. I'm not sure if openssl supports anything other than English, but if it does support other languages, setting the environment variable `LC_ALL=C` should take care of that problem.

@evansd @edmorley I realised that my use case is a bit wider than just serving index.html on `/`. I also needed support for frontend routing, using solutions such as [react-router](https://reacttraining.com/react-router/). I ended up extending the middleware a bit and overriding a few more WhiteNoise methods.

Since I understand that making this a part of WhiteNoise doesn't make sense based on what @evansd said about being wary of adding new features, I started a new project I named [django-spa](https://github.com/metakermit/django-spa). The idea would be to use WhiteNoise as a dependency (keep track of your development) and make sure that a typical use-case of serving single page apps on `/` (where all non-Django routing is delegated to the JS frontend) works (including things like 404s). To support this I made a 3-step decision which I made sure works both on development (DEBUG=False) and production deployments (`DEBUG=False`, serving files from the `self.files` dictionary):

1) try to find a static file either on `/` or `/static/` (or *indexl.html* if `/` is requested)
2) see if Django matches the url (so that Django admin and templates still work)
3) everything else gets routed to `/` (so that fronted routing can be used)

It's still early stage, as I need to make sure everything works on CDNs too. I'm testing it for an app of my own and plan to extend features as I bump into problems. Feedback welcome :)

Hello!

I am happy to hear you found some use for this little library.  Unfortunately, it is no longer maintained.  (I should really make a note of that somewhere, or remove this repo entirely.)  In the five+ years since I first wrote this library, I have grown to dislike the unjustified constraints it places on its users and would no longer recommend its use.

Funnily enough, argument parsing is part of the problem.  This library should not be responsible for parsing your command-line arguments.  It results in quirks like this one.  Users should be free to parse their arguments any way they please.  I would suppose my original (misguided) motivation for wrapping argument parsing functionality was to make it easy to solve simple problems.  As an unintended consequence of that design decision, this library made downright impossible to solve complex problems.  If your plugin configuration cannot be represented with the library's narrow-minded `add_option()` method then you are SOL.  This is poor design that I cannot easily change without breaking backwards compatibility.

You are, of course, free to fork this repository and modify it to suit your needs, though you would be better served by alternatives.  The [nagiosplugin](http://pythonhosted.org/nagiosplugin/tutorial/) library for Python appears to be of much better design, though I have no personal experience with it.  It is also available on the [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/nagiosplugin/).  Source code is available [here](https://bitbucket.org/flyingcircus/nagiosplugin/overview) but please check the [ZPL license terms](http://old.zope.org/Resources/License/ZPL-2.1) since they are slightly more restrictive than the BSD 2-clause terms that apply to this software.

These days I write Nagios plugins in Go and would recommend [olorin/nagiosplugin](https://github.com/olorin/nagiosplugin) for plugin development in that language.

I'm pretty sure this has to do with how the cache is being populated. As I said, tablesorter does not currently support `colspan` and `rowspan` in the tbody, unless it is inside of a info only tbody (class name set by the [`cssInfoBlock` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#cssinfoblock)) So the HTML should look like this to make it work properly: ```html ... showtime ... testgroup/td> ... ``` You can check the cache by opening the browser console and typing in the following: ```js $('table')[0].config.cache ``` And check the `normalized` portion of the cache to see if it is being populated properly.

I'VE FOUND A WORKAROUND TO MAKE IT WORK WITH ANACONDA!!

I'm trying to fix it or at least find out why does Anaconda have problems with Polyglot. But The problem is actually not Polyglot or pycld2. The problem is actually between libicu (operating system) and pyicu (python). If you execute `from icu import Locale` you get exactly the same error:

> /home/david/anaconda3/envs/py34/lib/python3.4/site-packages/_icu.cpython-34m.so: undefined symbol: _ZTIN6icu_5414LEFontInstanceE

What I've tried:

- Installing all `icu` packages available on conda (from all channels, for the version 54.1)
- Building the python package by my own from ICU's source code (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/PyICU/) with exactly the same results.
- Using the file xxx.so created by the operating system's package: this way it works fine!

So what's the workaround?

1. Install the operating system's packages: `sudo apt install python3-icu libicu57 icu-devtools` (package names may be different on other operating systems, I'm using Ubuntu 16.10)
2. Install python's icu with conda: `conda install icu=57.1` (it must be the same version your operating system supports, in my case it's 57.1)
3. Delete the .so file generated by conda installer: `rm ~/anaconda3/envs/py34/lib/python3.4/site-packages/_icu.cpython-34m.so`
4. Create a symbolic link to the .so file generated by the operating system package manager: `ln -s /usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/_icu.cpython-35m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so ~/anaconda3/envs/py34/lib/python3.4/site-packages/_icu.cpython-34m.so`

Please notice that I have an environment with Python3.4 on my computer, but Ubuntu's default python3 version is 3.5. It doesn't matter, it works anyway.

I think I didn't explain properly how white lists work, I apologize for that.

`white_list_pref` and `white_list_asn` are thought to add prefixes/ASN to the result of the IRR-data gathering process, so prefixes/ASNs listed there are treated exactly as those that are actually listed in IRR records.

The other white list, `white_list_route`, is the one I suggest to use to "manually" accept routes from peers. When a route should be rejected because it doesn't pass IRR-filters **but** it is actually accepted solely because it matches a `white_list_route` entry, if the general `filtering.irrdb.tag_as_set` option is set it is tagged with the `prefix_not_present_in_as_set` and `origin_not_present_in_as_set`.

So, if the route server is set with `enforce_[origin|prefix]_in_as_set` and a route with a `[origin|prefix]_not_present_in_as_set` community is seen it means that said route is accepted solely because of `white_list_route`.

Hopefully with the herein referenced commit I should have made it clearer in the docs and within the configuration files comments.

Personal notes from analyzing Python3 performance:

It appears that python 3 performance is overall similar to python 2 although distributed a bit differently - module loading seems to be a larger share (up to several hundred ms). That might be due to running within a Docker container for that portion, though. 

Overall, for Python 2 & 3, it looks like rough expected benchmarks (at least on this system with a fast SSD, so disk I/O is fast) are:

* ~100-200 ms to load & initialize python libraries - fairly constant with different testset sizes
* 10-100 ms to load & parse YAML files (including fairly complex test sets here) - increases with testset size and complexity, but still generally fast
* parsing headers is more expensive than expected, can be 5-10% of overall time for very fast local requests

The YAML parsing can be speeded up quite a bit by enabling access to libyaml bindings. 
[According to StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/16864132/is-there-an-up-to-date-fast-yaml-parser-with-python-bindings).  They that where pure-python YAML parsing takes 405 msec, libyaml version is 59.2 msec.

It looks like that would require some trivial PyRestTest changes (to conditionally import and use the yaml CLoader rather than)... however not worth dealing with unless parsing becomes a bottleneck.

Sample benchmark + analysis command:
```
python -m cProfile -o all-miniapp-nobench.profile pyresttest/resttest.py http://localhost:8000 examples/miniapp-run-all.yaml
snakeviz all-miniapp-nobench.profile
```

>  may be due to the initial processing prior to sending the first curl request

 **Installing libyaml may speed it up**, because pyyaml can use that for faster parsing of the YAML (possible bottleneck).

Yes, there is a fixed overhead to parsing and validating the test structure (it shouldn't be this much though), but it's a one-time cost and *should not* be that long unless your tests are extremely large or complex.

I would need to be able to replicate this and do some experimentation to see where the overheads are. 
Can you provide sample YAML and which version of Python, PyRestTest (from source or stable release from PIP), and what OS you're running? 

In general, once the initial parsing & configuration is done, the actual requests *should* be fairly fast, because [PyCurl's native code is considerably faster than requests](http://stackoverflow.com/a/32899936/95122) (link is to a benchmark I did).  There's some overhead in configuring and analyzing each request/response pair, of course. 

The benchmark methods are fairly optimized: there's a test for performance in there, which has shown that overhead is <0.3 ms per request on a fairly weak system.  I'll have to see if that has regressed. 

The test methods *haven't* been as optimized, because they do some additional processing and there hasn't been a need yet.  The curl handles could be reused (big performance gain), and there's some room for caching of templates (one smaller bottleneck).

```python
intdict = Dict(key_type=IntegerString(), value_types=String())
```

With this setup you won't hit this problem and that is the use case for which support for key types was added. The use case when your dictionary can have keys of different types is mostly for, well, serializing data represented by a fixed structure dictionaries (and thus keys are strings).

Ji, Josef. I believe the webrtc VAD looks at energy in different frequency bands over time, not just the total energy level in a frame.

In my experience, it will sometimes report false positives when it is first used (like from tapping on a microphone), but it seems to tune itself and give better results once it hears actual voice audio.

You could also try changing the aggressiveness parameter.

I'm not aware of any documentation on the webrtc VAD.  Most of what I've learned about it I got from reading comments in the [official source code](https://chromium.googlesource.com/external/webrtc/+/master/webrtc/common_audio/vad/).

## rules
`rules` element: groups all the rules. Contains one or more `rule` elements

## rule
`rule` element: a single rule. Contains one or more `sub` elements, and one or more `condition` elements. All conditions must evaluate to `true` for the rule to be
-  `name`: attribute, optional. String. Descriptive name for this rule.
- `enabled`: attribute, optional. Bool. If this rule should be evaluated or not.

## sub
`sub` element: a single glyph to glyph substitution. 
- `name` attribute, required. Name of the source glyph. 
- `byname` attribute, required. Name of the replacement glyph.

## condition
`condition` element: a single condition that has to evaluate to true for this rule to be valid.
- `tag` element: attribute, required. OpenType axis tag. 
- `minimum` attribute, required. Number.
- `maximum` attribute, required. Number.

Just a note, ChatterBot's storage adapters are not the same thing as Django's database backends. By default, ChatterBot will use it's built in _Django storage adapter_ which uses whatever database backend your Django settings are set up to use. So, if you use a Django storage backend like [Django MongoDB Engine](https://django-mongodb-engine.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) then ChatterBot will access your instance of MongoDB through that adapter directly (probably avoiding the error you encountered).

The first thought is fine if you're regularly polling. But I know that's not the case with POSIX `timer_create()`-based waits. (You just set it to go off on the wall clock and let the kernel handle it. Just sleep through the whole thing with no polling.)

So the underlying implementation has to hand out some kind of cancellation callable. But because of the synchronous nature, we need another way to return it.

I suppose converting it from functional to OOP would work? Make a `Sleeper` class with all the existing methods, plus a `.cancel()`. The module-level functions would just make a `Sleeper` and call the appropriate method.

If you wanted to keep objects and classes out of it, you could add a `cancel` argument which accepts a callable, which is called with the `cancel()` function as its argument. (If that explanation made any sense.)

Yes, you're correct on when the default factory is used: only when no explicit initial value is given.

Okay, I'll code that version up. I'll go with `field_with_default_factory()` for now, and when we get back to bikeshedding we can change it.

On a somewhat related note, what can PEP 484 do with:
```
    @dataclass
    class Foo:
        x: list = field(repr=False)
```
That is, where there's no type information at all in the call to `field()`? Would there be an override that specifies that when no `type` or `default` is given, that the return type is Any?

Note that `type` will only be specified in the dynamic `make_class()` scenario. In the normal `@dataclass` case, it's an error to specify it. So in the normal `@dataclass` case, the type information, if any, would have to come from the type of the `default` value.

Hi, I just rebased as requested. Travis now works but all build fail even though the code works well in practice both under linux and windows. I suspect this is because:

- The tests include special characters which are currently not supported on Windows, such Page Up and Page Down,
- The tests do not take the different ways in which keys are handled under Windows or Linux into consideration, as discussed in #9   e.g. `if (inp == key.DOWN or inp == "down")`

Again, I would suggest making the Linux code work like the new Windows code instead of the opposite. The way it's done for Windows, it is easy to both compare a key, and print it:

```
if inp == "down":
    print('you pressed' + inp)
```

but this is not so easy on Linux since the special characters are not translated to a readable string.

I took a look at the parsing code.  There seem to be several situations where linebreaks really ought to be considered tokens, but are not.  This results in parsing that differs from MediaWiki.  For instance, in Media Wiki, if you have (admittedly perverse) markup like this:

```
Some stuff with ''formatting with newline
in it'' and other stuff
```

Then what you get is

> Some stuff with *formatting with newline* in it *and other stuff*

That is, a line break ends the italic formatting.  We also need the line break to end the list item.

def parse(url, extra_schemes=None):

What is clear here is that homegear uses a queue and will keep retrying at least for some time until the devices received the packet, so even though HASS gets an ERROR "No answer from device" from pyhomematic, that doesn't have to mean the packet never arrives. 

I am not sure therefore if we should be acting on this. I think "No answer from device" should be a WARN and an ERROR should only come if Homegear gives up and drops it from the queue, but I'm not sure if that is even happening or how to handle it. I have not had any messages that never got applied in my current logs. 

Maybe CCU handles this differently? Does it queue as well? I think if it does we can close this as a NOOP as I think the core issue was in HASS losing connection with Homegear, and this is just noise on the line.

The reason that you have having these errors is because the password that you are using is misidentified by passlib. This can happen when your password choice happens to start with one of the strings that passlib uses to identifying hashing schemes  e.g. $5$, $6$, $2$, $2a$, $2b$, $2x$, $2y$.

This causes Flask-Security to think that your password was saved using a hash and not plaintext.
```python
if _pwd_context.identify(user.password) != 'plaintext':
        password = get_hmac(password)
```
As you can see Flask now thinks that the saved user.password is encoded with a hash (even if it's not) and therefore that it was salted (even if it's not). So in order to check your input password against the user.password in the system, it salts your input password. That is why it throws the "no salt" error. So you are correct in saying that the error message is 'incorrect' but Flask-Security /is/ legitimately trying to salt your password.

You might ask: So given passlib's proclivity for false positives, why do we use it to identify password hashes at all? Isn't the the SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH flag enough to tell Flask-Security whether user.password is encoded in plaintext? Well technically yes, but the reason that passlib's identify function is used is so that Flask-Security can gracefully deal with hashing algorithm changes i.e. if you change SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH between runs, this identify call allows Flask-Security to load passwords using older hashing schemes and rehash it using your new hashing scheme.

This bug is more than just the error message, if you had a salt defined then Flask-Security would mistakenly salt your input password and it passlib would also misidentify the stored password as having been hashed rather than plaintext which may lead to a decoding error in passlib.

How can this be fixed? We can't just change the error message or suppress the error for the reason given above. The source of the error is in a fundamental ambiguity in the way that password hashes are done. Because the hashing method is not stored with the password, after a hash has been written it's impossible to know whether it was a plaintext password that looks like a hash or actually a hash. The easiest method imo is to output some kind of identifying string in front of plaintext passwords e.g. $plaintext$. But this could potentially be breaking for old code running on databases that don't have the identifying string (which might be avoidable with some backwards-compatibility checks though I haven't thought it through completely yet). 

An alternative is to just make SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH=plaintext indicate a second plaintext check. That is to say when config is set to plaintext, Flask-Security would put a try-catch around verify_and_update_password and in case of failure (possible misidentification), would just do a string match between the input password and the stored password without using passlib. This is a potential security flaw since someone with access to the hashed user.password would be able to unlock the account without the extra layer of security provided by the salt. But since plaintext mode is obviously insecure to begin with, this might not be horrible.

It might also be desirable to just store the password hash type in the db rather than relying on the identifier. But this also breaks backwards compatibility in a less comfortable way since it also forces a change to the db schema.

There are probably other workarounds that I haven't thought of yet.

1. It is not necessary to change the signature for `MailChimpClient.__init__()` in order to allow the change you suggested, and maintaining the existing signature prevents someone upgrading from the last version to the current version from needing to recode some of their applications just because someone decided the signature needed to be changed. There is no functional reason why the signature needs to be changed, so leaving it as is causes the least number of issues to those that use this package, and this is a rare component to be changed, so will not add undue burden to the maintainers.
2. Some users of this package may decide that the use of multiple username strings are better, and may have some kind of logging built around the application. Again, as you said, there is no functional difference if it is removed, meaning there is no functional difference if it is left, and as it is allowed, it is implemented to provide the largest array of features to the users of this package.
3. Requiring the use of the keyword argument only applies for users that choose not to use the currently available positional arguments as they exist today, meaning that only those that choose to ignore the user field will need to use it. This does not make the package more difficult, and in fact is codified under the second axiom of PEP-20, "Explicit is better than implicit", as good practice when writing Python code. This is also the reason that the names of the existing keyword arguments should not be changed, as those that already use the package may use the positional form, or they may explicitly call the keyword arguments, and it is in our interest to make the transition from one version to another as smooth as possible. This package follows semantic versioning, and the changes proposed involving the removal of the data and changing of the keyword argument names constitutes a backwards incompatible version change. Please take a moment to read the specifics on semantic versioning [here](http://semver.org/), specifically spec item [8](http://semver.org/#spec-item-8).

Calling `make` from the parent process results in all the 4 environments having the same PID and thread name and thus the pipe to the emulator is the same for all of them. It appears that standard Atari Envs work with your configuration so I'll take a look at their source to check how they initialize their pipes to Stella. For now, if you can refactor your code to call `make` from within the subprocess, it will work. i.e.

```python
import gym_super_mario_bros
import multiprocessing as mp


class Env(object):
    def __init__(self, env):
        self.env = env
        self.env.reset()
        self.done = False

    def run(self):
        for step in range(5000):
            if self.done:
                state = self.env.reset()
                self.done = False
            state, reward, self.done, info = self.env.step(self.env.action_space.sample())


def runner():
    env = Env(gym_super_mario_bros.make('SuperMarioBros-v0'))
    env.run()


procs = []

for idx in range(4):
    proc = mp.Process(target=runner)
    proc.start()
    procs.append(proc)

for proc in procs:
    proc.join()
```

Hi! In the `post` method you have written you do not create the inline forms or call the `forms_valid` method. Creating the inline forms with the appropriate methods will process the file data from the inline forms submitted in the request. It looks like you've used the wrong version of `post` as the basis for your method. 
Take a look at [ProcessFormWithInlinesView.post](https://github.com/AndrewIngram/django-extra-views/blob/master/extra_views/advanced.py#L88). Your code would probably work fine if you didn't override `post` but instead set instance.author in [forms_valid()](https://github.com/AndrewIngram/django-extra-views/blob/master/extra_views/advanced.py#L46).

@grant-humphries - What I meant was that if you had data/schema that went 8 layers deep but returned only 4, the result wouldn't be very usable as it's missing data and if you used that to edit the data the end result would save only 4 layers and truncate the rest.  So, I think it should be an exception and leave it up to the implementer to deal with it as they want.  In other words, it should always be the case that `objectify(dictify(x))` should always return `x` if there's no exception.

In your specific example, you'd specify the limit to recursion to be 4 since you expect nothing to be deeper than that.  Anything that went deeper would be an exception.

Hi there -- I'm having trouble thinking of a situation where request.session and the local session variable could or would be different since existing sessions are based on the current request and session cookie (and there can only be one session cookie per pyramid_redis_sessions config, based on the cookie name/cookie domain/etc).

If you're trying to add support in your application for multiple sessions, you might consider creating multiple pyramid_redis_sessions factories (each with their own unique config) and then writing your own session factory that would dispatch to the appropriate one based on what you need. If I'm understanding you this seems like something that should live in the app and not this library.

If I've misunderstood though please let me know what your use case is, and if possible provide a small reproducible example showing how the current process is broken.

@pipermerriam so it turns out that **without** `metaclass=ABCMeta` only `mypy` will catch the error whereas with `metaclass=ABCMeta` both `mypy` and `python` will catch the error.

See:

```python

from abc import abstractmethod

class C():
    @abstractmethod
    def my_abstract_method(self):
        print('foo')

class D(C):
    pass

x = C()
y = D()
```

```shell
$ mypy test.py 
test.py:13: error: Cannot instantiate abstract class 'C' with abstract attribute 'my_abstract_method'
test.py:15: error: Cannot instantiate abstract class 'D' with abstract attribute 'my_abstract_method'
```
but 

```shell
$ python3 test.py
$
```

Wheras with that, `python` will catch the error as well.

```python
from abc import ABCMeta, abstractmethod

class C(metaclass=ABCMeta):
    @abstractmethod
    def my_abstract_method(self):
        print('foo')

class D(C):
    pass

x = C()

y = D()
```

```shell
$ python3 test.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 11, in <module>
    x = C()
TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class C with abstract methods my_abstract_method
```

> I've been interested in exploring using a relational db (like sqlite) for some of this structured metadata:
> - txn hash -> block hash, txn_index
> - canonical block number -> block hash
> - canonical chain head
> - block_hash -> total difficulty
> - canonical header chain with just a limited subset of the header data: (hash, number, ??)

 
 It looks like geth is using leveldb to build the relationship:

#### 1. Store blockReceipts body in one entry:
In py-evm, storing receipt:
https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/5525c12fd1e1860fe5e885d68b9eb4d38009a833/evm/db/chain.py#L598-L601

In geth, storing the entire `BlockReceipt` into a single entry with prefix (`blockReceiptsPrefix`) instead of separate receipts key/value:
https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/blob/7605e63cb9dda7fc5bb619abf1eb1f74ac3f6cc1/core/database_util.go#L464-L480

#### 2. Store block body in one key-value store includes both transactions and uncles
When they want to access a transaction, they have to look up `blockHash, blockNumber, txIndex` via transaction hash, get the block body, and then get the specific transaction:
https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/blob/7605e63cb9dda7fc5bb619abf1eb1f74ac3f6cc1/core/database_util.go#L284-L295

#### 3. Rough comparision
Something geth doesn’t store, but py-evm does:
1. The way store receipts: in trie db, each receipt takes one key-value store.
2. The way store body:
	1. Uncle: in trie db, each uncle takes one key-value store
	2. Tx: in trie db, each tx takes one key-value store

Something py-evm doesn’t store, but geth does:
1. Meta data
	1. block_hash -> total difficulty
	2. block_hash -> block_number 
	3. block_number + block_hash -> block receipts
	4. receipt_hash -> transaction/receipt lookup metadata (block_hash, block_number, transaction_index)   # py-evm only has txHash -> blockHash
	5. bloom bits
2. The way store receipts: one key-value store per block
3. The way store body: one key-value store per block

---
It’s still unclear for me to compare the trade-off between more key-value stores for the actual data and more lookups, but the way we get a specific block may be inefficient when other nodes want to download data from Trinity. It seems Trinity has to query and decode the block body from every transactions and uncles to rebuild the block body:
https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/f7bc89f7c05bcc2e721cacf56408f1bf821ad887/evm/vm/forks/frontier/blocks.py#L88-L104

As specified, the [`IAgent.request`](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/16.0.0/api/twisted.web.iweb.IAgent.html#request) method that Treq builds upon specifies that `uri` is bytes.

We can't change documented interfaces within Twisted, because that's an incompatible change; specifically, it means that someone implementing `IAgent` _outside_ of Twisted would see their code break if new code were to pass it a completely legitimate value according to the new definition of the interface.

However, `treq.get` et. al. are not part of any formally-specified interface, so it would be legit for them to just start accepting unicode.

Later on, it is perfectly reasonable for existing concrete classes to start implementing new interfaces, and an interface may have a name conflict IFF one is a pure superset of the other's signature (takes new parameters or accepts new types).  So all the `IAgent` implementations in Twisted could grow an `ITextURIAgent` implementation if we define that new interface as responsible for URL handling.

In the meanwhile, the docstring for `URL` nearly gives you the recipe here: `URL.fromString(someUserInput).asURI().encode('ascii')` will give you the bytes to pass to `treq.request`; this implements not just internationalized domain names, but also all other aspects of internationalized URLs, i.e. non-ascii segments.

So this should be pretty low-hanging fruit for potential new contributors, assuming we're willing to drop Twisted before 16.0, which I think at this point is OK (see #98).

AFAIK no operator in sklearn uses `sample_weights` without having a `fit` function. In fact, we couldn't use an operator in sklearn if it didn't have a `fit` function because it wouldn't work in a sklearn pipeline. Furthermore, because we use sklearn pipelines as our solution representation, sklearn pipeline only use `fit` and `prediction`/`transform` separately.

>Also I wanted to know if sample_weights is used in tpot's scoring function, which determines preferences between pipelines?

No, the `sample_weights` are not used in the scoring function unless you separately tell TPOT to use a scoring function that takes sample weights into account. Even in sklearn, setting `sample_weights` affects only the training process of the algorithms, not their evaluation.

> So the high watermark is the offset of the most recent message in the partition that a producer produced? 
Yes
>And the low watermark is the oldest message in the partition, even if it is days old? 
Yes
> The low watermark will only ever change if the broker ages a message out to save space, regardless of what the consumer consumes or doesn't consume, or commits or doesn't commit, right?
Yes, according to the configured topic retention policy.

> From what you say, it sounds as if statistics.interval.ms controls how often the client issues some kind of a request over the network to the broker. I had imagined that somehow I was only controlling the rate of some internal query with that.

Fetching the low watermark requires an explicit protocol request, and it is probably overkill to fetch it every statistics.interval.ms (MAX(statistics_interval_ms, 60s) would probably be more than sufficient), but I don't think this is much of a concern unless you set statistics.interval.ms really low (sub-second).

> Moreover, are you saying that even if my consumer does a poll(3.0), under the covers there are frequent fetches from the consumer to the broker? Can I control their frequency, or just how quickly the broker responds to them with fetch.wait.max.ms?

Yes, poll() only checks the internal queue for new messages/callbacks. librdkafka will pre-fetch messages in the background to fullfill queued.min.messages or queued.max.messages.kbytes. The idea is that poll() should always be ready to return a new message to the application without having to perform a network request.  fetch.wait.max.ms is actually applied on the broker, making the FetchRequest block that long on the broker if there are no more messages to fetch - think of it as a long poll.

try:

}


def parallelize(func, iterator, n_jobs, extra):
    extra = tuple(extra)
    return Parallel(n_jobs=n_jobs)(delayed(func)(*(item + extra)) for item in iterator)


def iter_comments(loc):
    with bz2.BZ2File(loc) as file_:
        for i, line in enumerate(file_):
            yield ujson.loads(line)['body']


pre_format_re = re.compile(r'^[\`\*\~]')
post_format_re = re.compile(r'[\`\*\~]$')
url_re = re.compile(r'\[([^]]+)\]\(%%URL\)')
link_re = re.compile(r'\[([^]]+)\]\(https?://[^\)]+\)')
def strip_meta(text):
    text = link_re.sub(r'\1', text)
    text = text.replace('>', '>').replace('<', '<')
    text = pre_format_re.sub('', text)
    text = post_format_re.sub('', text)
    return text


def load_and_transform(batch_id, in_loc, out_dir):
    out_loc = path.join(out_dir, '%d.txt' % batch_id)
    if path.exists(out_loc):
        return None
    print('Batch', batch_id)
    nlp = spacy.load("en")
    #nlp = spacy.en.English(parser=False, tagger=False, matcher=False, entity=False)
    with io.open(out_loc, 'w', encoding='utf8') as out_file:
        with io.open(in_loc, 'rb') as in_file:
            for byte_string in Doc.read_bytes(in_file):
                doc = Doc(nlp.vocab).from_bytes(byte_string)
                doc.is_parsed = True
                out_file.write(transform_doc(doc)) 


def parse_and_transform(batch_id, input_, out_dir):
    out_loc = path.join(out_dir, '%d.txt' % batch_id)
    if path.exists(out_loc):
        return None
    print('Batch', batch_id)
    nlp = spacy.load("en")
    nlp.matcher = None
    with io.open(out_loc, 'w', encoding='utf8') as file_:
        for text in input_:
            file_.write(transform_doc(nlp(strip_meta(text))))


def transform_doc(doc):
    for ent in doc.ents:
        ent.merge(ent.root.tag_, ent.text, LABELS[ent.label_])
    for np in doc.noun_chunks:
        while len(np) > 1 and np[0].dep_ not in ('advmod', 'amod', 'compound'):
            np = np[1:]
        np.merge(tag = np.root.tag_, lemma= np.text, ent_type =np.root.ent_type_)
    strings = []
    for sent in doc.sents:
        if sent.text.strip():
            strings.append(' '.join(represent_word(w) for w in sent if not w.is_space))
    if strings:
        return '\n'.join(strings) + '\n'
    else:
        return ''


def represent_word(word):
    if word.like_url:
        return '%%URL|X'
    text = re.sub(r'\s', '_', word.text)

    if not tag:

    return text + '|' + tag

    parallelize(do_work, enumerate(jobs), n_workers, [out_dir])

Very interested in this as well. I think I could throw together a solution but something tells me the folks at Explosion have a much more elegant method. Given a string, if it is a match for the spelling of a set of word meanings then choose the most frequent POS tag for that spelling from the corpus. For example I'd assume "brown" would go to ADJ rather than NOUN. I'm pretty sure this is what the folks at Explosion are doing. 

A hack work around to this would be to use a dictionary library, check if the word is in the dictionary, grab it's part of speech and then pass it on. The problem is that the exact beauty of sense2vec is it's ability to interpret words that aren't in the dictionary. 

If we fail to find the word in the dictionary we could then try to take a pretty stupid approach and just try/catch with each of the POS until something works.

> The fundamental issue that I have with @preflight is that the timing of it is relative to something conceptually outside of the reactive framework but it is expected to block processing of handlers while it runs.

Yes, in OOP terms, the underlying hook mechanics leak into the reactive layers. We'd rather not have those leaks because of complexity and because we'd like to get the reactive framework in places where hooks don't exist.

> The concept of "mutually exclusive flags," or even "pre-set callbacks" on flags fits better with the framework, in that sense, to me. However, both end up interrupting the conceptual flow of "when a flag is changed, the handlers that care about it are queued to run,"

I'm not sure what the right concept is. Is it possible that the queue is just not the correct concept here? Alex had a suggestion about this, and although I don't completely agree with his proposal, he might be right that the queue is the conceptual issue. I don't agree because his solution would prevent handlers from running multiple times. I think this is necessary and even wanted in some cases, like in the case of an update status hook. My proposal and more info about this at the end of this comment.

> so they both still make me a bit uneasy. But, obviously there's a definite use-case for it, so my preference would be for the descriptive approach ("these flags are mutually exclusive"). The flag classes should give a good place to implement the behavior without it being at the module level.


>
> > Should handlers be reinvoked if the data attached to their flags changes?
>
> I definitely think we'd need to be able to mark flags as "changed" based on data or some other condition, even if they're already set.
>

Can we treat "the flag data changes" exactly like "this flag was unset and set again"? That seems logical to me..


# [proposal] Two sets instead of queue

You have two sets of handlers: `dormant` and `handled`. All handlers start in the `dormant` set.

1. Test each dormant handler and execute the first one that matches.
2. Put the handler in the `handled` set.
3. Check the `handled` set for handlers whose preconditions are false.
4. Go back to 1, start at *the beginning* of the `dormant` set.

What we get with this:

 - We get control over the order that handlers are executed.
   - We can eliminate the *"`restart` handler causes multiple restarts"* issue by putting the restart handler at the end of the file.
   - We get the wanted behavior of *"the `status_set` handler gets executed each time the status changes"* by putting that handler first.
- The order in which the handlers are executed will always be the same -> reproducible.
- Handlers are sure that their preconditions are met when they are executed. -> Less checking of state necessary.

The order in the dormant set is the order in the layers and files. The highest handler in the base layer is the first handler in the `dormant` set. The lowest handler in the top-level layer is the last handler in the `dormant` set. This would mean that we can put a `status_set` handler in the base layer that is executed each time when any layer changes the status (changes the object of the status flag).


Thoughts?

*PS: I don't think that this is harder to understand than what the current concept is, especially since this proposal doesn't leak the "hooks" concept at all. Handlers aren't re-executed in a following hook unless they get thrown out of the "handled" set.*

I want to put it on the table again. As I remember it was a proposal, and I want to put forward an idea about bringing these tests as a test-suite. We could take these approach from LLVM community, where there are internal unit-tests and external test-suite. 

My two proposals:
1. External Tool, similar to LNT in LLVM
    1. Get tests from the cpython as a test-suite and store them separately from the whole project, cleaning it up before, because these tests include some non-python files
    2. We will add some tool that will run RedBaron against these test-suite, as LNT tool for LLVM. From my point of view, there is a problem with this approach. We have to distribute and support external tool. 

2. Part of internal tests
    1. It is a bit simpler to distribute with the RedBaron. Because all tests bring together into a package. Indeed, we should keep in a mind, that it's a massive amount of source files.
    2. We will create a test case, that will read and parse a file, one by one. Afterwards, it will show up a statistics about success and failures during the parsing.

And last but not least, we could investigate the problems with concrete sources after. But code coverage over the language is the first problem, if I may say so.

Hi @oliver-zhou, thanks for your report! There is already such a mechanism, but the readme lacks its description.

- `DEBUG` is set to the value of your Algolia setting `RAISE_EXCEPTIONS` and defaults to Django's `settings.DEBUG`.

See the `ChangeLog` of V1.3.0:
```
  * [ADD] Option to raise or not exceptions. By default, raise only when DEBUG=True
```

And the code of `settings.py` defining `settings.DEBUG`:
```python
from django.conf import settings

SETTINGS = settings.ALGOLIA
DEBUG = SETTINGS.get('RAISE_EXCEPTIONS', settings.DEBUG)
```

If you want to disable the raising of exceptions, simply set RAISE_EXCEPTIONS=False in your settings:

```python
ALGOLIA = {
    'APPLICATION_ID': 'MyAppID',
    'API_KEY': 'MyApiKey',
    'RAISE_EXCEPTIONS': False
}
```

I'll close this feature request as the feature is already implemented, and will improve its documentation in the readme. Let me know if this works for you!

after talking to engineering, I realise I'm mixing up things and created a confusion.
0x8108 is not for redirect-to-IP but it's a redirect-to-RT with the RT expressed in the IPv4 form:
In our CLI format, it's the third of this list:
> RP/0/0/CPU0:XRv1-demo(config-pmap-c)#redirect nexthop route-target ?
>   <1-65535>:           2-byte AS number
>   <65536-4294967295>:  4-byte AS number in asplain format
>   A.B.C.D:             IPv4 address
> RP/0/0/CPU0:XRv1-demo(config-pmap-c)#

So it's not https://www.ietf.org/id/draft-ietf-idr-flowspec-redirect-rt-bis-04.txt that should be used here.
0x08 is indeed currently used in our implementation.

Maybe I should create a new issue for this, but a related idea which may be helpful for your is to also have a callback for an allocation function of zeroed memory (i.e., calloc instead of malloc).  The default implementation could just call malloc and memcpy, but calling calloc can be a bit faster since the OS often keeps around pages of pre-zeroed memory.

I'm not sure how much of a performance boost (if any) brotli would see, but I saw a pretty huge improvement in crush when I switched from malloc+memset to calloc… I think it's a bigger benefit for larger allocations because you don't have the cache hit from moving stuff into the CPU just to zero it.

I've been doing some profiling of brotli on 32-bit arm, and found that the following code change can reduce the number of loads we do in the hot symbol preloading/decoding code path, which is good for an average 1.5% increase in decompression performance on recent out-of-order Arm cores, and an average 5% increase in decompression performance on recent in-order Arm cores. This is testing using the test cases found in the test suite for snappy (https://github.com/google/snappy/tree/master/testdata).

I'd be interested to know if you have any other thoughts on potential avenues for optimisation of brotli on arm. I see that you've already used NEON to accelerate the CommandPostDecodeLiterals stage (the memmove16 optimisation), but otherwise there doesn't seem to be much else that I can see that is both amenable to vectorisation here, but also particularly hot.

Yes, handling these arguments in _ParseKeywordArgs sounds reasonable to me.
You could modify _ParseKeywordArgs to return `kwargs, remaining_args, unused_kwargs` instead of  `kwargs, remaining_args`, where unused_args is a list of any keyword args that weren't used.
By separating these unused keyword args from `remaining_args`, we prevent these args that were intended to be keyword args from accidentally being used as positional args.
Then, at the end of _ParseFn, we can add the unused_kwargs back into remaining_args, in case they're used later on in _Fire.

conda install -n tsfresh numpy==1.10.4

FastText always uses spaces as token delimiters (and thus, will not work for non-tokenized sequences). @rok987 this issue.

The indexes piece will be changing, at which point the only argument for an entity will be a Key... Further, the index specification is rarely used, and not very well defined in its current form (hence why we'll be changing it).

IMO, an Entity is just the Key with data, so I don't think this pushes us in the direction of an ORM, but just makes it more convenient to do the common cases.

> What is a parent if there is no such thing as a key?

A parent can be another Entity (that's how a lot of people think of it anyway -- few people realize you can have many keys all with the same "parent" where the parent entity actually doesn't exist).

Breaking this down into the kind of stuff I want to do:

1. Create a new entity of a specific kind and an auto-assigned ID
1. Create a new entity with a specific kind, auto-assigned ID, and specific parent
1. Create a new entity with a specific key
1. Create a new entity with a specific key and specific parent
1. Look-up an entity with a specific key and do something with it (save it? delete it?)
1. Run a query which brings back lots of entities and do something with those (look at them? update them? delete them?)

The code I'd want to write for each would be:

```python
from gcloud import datastore

# Create a new entity, auto-assigned ID.
person = datastore.Entity('Person')
# Note that I don't want to do: `person = datastore.Entity(datastore.Key('Person'))`
# because it feels a bit backwards to create a Key that isn't really a Key -- it's a partial key... 
person['name'] = 'Danny'
datastore.put(person)

# Create a new entity with a specific kind, auto-assigned ID, and specific parent
parent = datastore.Entity('Person')
parent['name'] = 'Parent'
datastore.put(parent)
child = datastore.Entity('Person', parent=parent) # Note, not parent.key
child['name'] = 'Child'
datastore.put(child)

# Create a new entity with a specific key
person = datastore.Entity('Person', 'dhermes')
# Note this is also OK IMO: `person = datastore.Entity(key=datastore.Key('Person', 'dhermes'))`
person['name'] = 'Danny'
datastore.put(person)

# Create a new entity with a specific key and specific parent
parent = datastore.Entity('Person', 'parent')
parent['name'] = 'Parent'
child = datastore.Entity('Person', 'child', parent=parent)
child['name'] = 'Child'
datastore.put([parent, child])

# Look-up an entity
person = datastore.get(datastore.Key('Person', 1234))
person2 = datastore.get(datastore.Key('Person', 'Danny'))

# Run a query
query = datastore.Query('Person').add_filter('name', '=', 'Danny')
for person in query:
  person['active'] = True
  datastore.put(person)
```

Everyone that's using Cmd2 is probably doing:
  from Cmd2 import Cmd

We probably need something like this in __init__.py:
  from Cmd2 import Cmd

That way when they import from the new Cmd2 directory it'll provide Cmd in the location they expect.

Otherwise everything will break for everyone when we release the next version.

Hi @mgiaco 

The "commands at invocation" feature that allows you to run commands when you start the app by adding them on the command line works with all commands not just load or "@".  So you should be able to do something like "python3 yourapp.py 'pyscript your_script.py'".  You just need to put the command plus its arguments in quotes so the parser knows that is a single command.

I think it would be a better idea to run your commands within the Python script directly using syntax like **cmd('command_name command_args')** instead of loading the commands from commands.txt.  Look at the example Python script here for an example of what I'm talking about: https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2/blob/master/examples/scripts/conditional.py

I hope that answers your questions.

Good luck,

Todd

Sorry for the delay.

This one brings one issue I would like to solve, but goes deeper in the architecture: how to encode the data states into the schematics Model.

To understand, this is how schematics sees the *Data States* now:
- Unsafe (input data)
- Converted
- Validated
- Serialized (output data)

Unsafe and Serialized data are both "outside" the Model, that is, the object does not allocate storage for them (Unsafe is processed on `init` (and also on `import` and `validate`), and data in the Serialized state is only ever output by the `serialize` function).

To encode all data state transitions in the Model, it has to know in what state the data is in to take the appropriate action, according to the following state machine:

```
[Start State] [Transition] [Next State]
[Unsafe]    - convert   -> [Converted]
[Converted] - mutate    -> [Converted]
[Converted] - mutate    -> [Untrusted]
[Converted] - validate  -> [Validated]
[Validated] - serialize -> [Trusted]
```

The current Model has a combined dict (ChainMap) that stores the Converted + Validated states, and special cases access to the Converted data in some functions to select the correct transition dispatch. This already makes the code complex. Further storing the Untrusted stated in this architecture, will require some more special handling - not by much though, I think, see my proposal in PR #484.

What I am thinking: if we instead encode the state with the data, this information can be pushed through the pipeline instead of being sorted outside in the Model. For example, store all data as a tuple (or instance) with `(STATE, data)`, the Model will require a simple dict, and the Schematics functions can make sure the transitions are stepped through the state machine. This would make data handling safer and potentially improve performance by allowing to skip re-conversion/re-validation in some cases.

After tinkering around a bit , I wrote my own subclass that takes in a list of functions that work on an input : 

```
class CleanedStringType(StringType):
    """
    A string type converter with the added functionality of allowing
    arbitrary transformations on the input.

    Usage :
        my_field = CleanedStringType(coverters=[lambda x : x.tolower()])
    """
    converters = []

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        """
        This takes in all the inputs as String Type, but takes in an extra
        input called converters.

        Converters must be a list of functions, and each of those functions
        must take in exactly 1 value , and return the transformed input
        :param kwargs:
        """
        if 'converters' in kwargs:
            self.converters = kwargs['converters']
        del kwargs['converters']
        super().__init__(**kwargs)

    def validate(self, value, context=None):
        for func in self.converters:
            value = func(value)
        return super().validate(value, context)
```

I too the freedom to take your code and merge it with what I had for batched mxnet topk. How about adding a function in utils.py that looks like this:
```python
def topk(scores: mx.nd.NDArray, k: int, batch_size: int, t: int, use_mxnet_topk: bool):
    # scores: (batch_size * beam_size, target_vocab_size)
    if t == 1:  # beam size is effectively 1
        folded_scores = scores[::k, :]
    else:
        # (batch_size, beam_size * vocab_size)
        folded_scores = scores.reshape((batch_size, k * scores.shape[-1]))

    if use_mxnet_topk:
        values, indices = mx.nd.topk(folded_scores, axis=1, k=k, ret_typ='both', is_ascend=True)
        best_hyp_indices, best_word_indices = np.unravel_index(indices.astype(np.int32).asnumpy().ravel(), scores.shape)
        values = values.reshape((-1,))
    else:
        folded_scores = folded_scores.asnumpy()
        # Get the scores
        # Indexes into folded_scores: (batch_size, beam_size)
        flat_idxs = np.argpartition(folded_scores, range(k))[:, :k]
        # Score values: (batch_size, beam_size)
        values = folded_scores[np.arange(folded_scores.shape[0])[:, None], flat_idxs].ravel()
        best_hyp_indices, best_word_indices = np.unravel_index(flat_idxs.ravel(), scores.shape)
```

Not quite. If in the second suite file you do not specify screenshot_root_directory, Robot Framework will see that you used different arguments than the last time the library was imported. In this case Robot Framework will create a new instance of Selenium2Library. Since it is a new instance, the library will use the default path (where the test log is written to). Every time you import a library, Robot Framework remembers what arguments where used to import the library. If the arguments where the same, then the global library instance is re-used. Otherwise a new instance is created with the new parameters.

Right, I took another look at this and refactored the build a little bit. 

The core of the problem is that the ocaml toplevel does not support dynamic linking and the -custom option on ocamlmklib doesn't add a custom runtime, but it simply disables dynamic linking.  I remember getting the toplevel to work at some point, but I have no recollection of how I did that; it's quite possible I played around with static libraries at the time. 

Here are some options: 
 - Add optional static libraries and make it a requirement for the ML API, or
 - automatically build a new toplevel that links Z3 dynamically, or
 - don't support bytecode ocaml at all, only native, or
 - figure out a way to load a shared object from the toplevel (hack ocamlrun?)
 - anything else?

Assuming that some system package will install a shared object for z3 (libz3.so) it would be very confusing for users if there is another, static library which is used for bytecode, but not in native mode, as they would see different errors and solver behaviour if those two libraries aren't precisely matched. That's why I don't like the static route.

I like the custom toplevel way, but mostly because it's probably the least amount of work for me. I have no idea whether anyone would ever use it. 

Not supporting bytecode is OK with me, but I guess there are some users who do use that at least for debugging. 

If there's a way to teach the toplevel to load a shared object let me know, I couldn't find one so far, so I assume adding facilities for that would be quite a lot of work.

There is an internal API on the Solver object, used by SPACER, that allows using temporary clauses.
It is called check_sat_cc.
Besides taking a cube (assumptions), the solver object can take a set of temporary clauses.
The advantage of this API is that the set of learned clauses are not going to be affected
by the temporary clauses. It isn't exposed as it is only partially implemented and it would take more than one use case to expose.

`@name` is a CLI feature to load a value from file, most useful for loading JSON that is painful and awkward to type on the command line. The reason it parses `this=@that` is because argparse allows space or = as a separator between an argument and its value and we needed to preserve the ability for `--arg=@filename` to work correctly.

We tried a couple different implementations and the current one is simplified for performance reasons. Based on the format of your connection string, we may need a more complex regex implementation.
@pchoudhari

Hey @colemickens! Long time no see. I actually don't work in Azure anymore, but I agree that the information should get out there. For #1 and #2, I threw together a couple blog posts that cover the topics:

Updating a scale set (including details around how POST APIs like reimage operate at the control plane): https://negatblog.wordpress.com/2018/06/05/updating-a-scale-set/
Reimaging VMs in a scale set (mostly focused on the data plane - what does reimage actually do?): https://negatblog.wordpress.com/2018/06/05/reimaging-vms-in-a-scale-set/

As for #3: It depends on exact context, but I suspect "upgrade" refers to "manual upgrade" (a.k.a. "update instances"...sigh, there are too many ways to describe the same thing). When doing PUT/PATCH updates in manual upgrade mode, only the scale set model is modified; existing VMs are not. Doing a "manual upgrade" call then brings up each VM specified in the request up to date with the latest model (note: the "manual upgrade" call itself is a POST API, so it behaves as discussed in the first blog post; in particular, this means that the operation happens on all VMs specified at the same time; the upgrade policy is bypassed). 

"rolling-update" likely refers to the "rolling" upgrade mode. In this mode, PUT/PATCH updates are rolled out to VMs in the scale set automatically in batches. In this mode, "manual upgrade" calls are not necessary.

Finally, "reimage" replaces the old, crusty OS disk of a specified set of VMs with new, shiny OS disks. (note: this is also a POST API, so it has the same behavior described above).

Hope this helps! Let me know if you have follow-up questions :)

Problem when installing with different versions of Debian distro occurs, like "jessie".
What worked for me. Change the following line
```bash 
echo "deb [arch=amd64] https://packages.microsoft.com/repos/azure-cli/ $AZ_REPO main" | \
     sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/azure-cli.list
```
to
```bash
echo "deb [arch=amd64] https://packages.microsoft.com/repos/azure-cli/ wheezy main" | \
     sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/azure-cli.list
```

Essentially we are telling the source file, to use a lower version of Linux Distribution for installing https.

Yea, I noticed it through a Twython import.

Looking at the original issue, this is due to oauthlib rissing MissingTokenError if 'access_token' is not part of the response body. It doesn't handle the 'error' provided by Facebook as it is not a known oauth2 error (as per spec); causing errors.raise_from_error in oauthlib not to raise an exception. The next fall-through there is the missing token exception. The error handling in oauthlib could probably be improved, both by adding support for registering custom errors (right now this has to be done by monkey-patching raise_from_error) and by having smarter handling about an 'error' key with an unsupported error. Technically that's against the oauth spec so it should become an 'invalid response' error, but with pluggable error handling requests-oauthlib could add a compliance fix for Facebook.

> An alternative implementation might be to add a `default_sources_glob` and a `sources` argument.

Adding a `source` - singular - optional argument to `PythonRequirementLibrary` might make sense to take the place of the dependency `python_requirements` injects. The tradeoff here is a wonky public BUILD dictionary `source` field that users should never use (see 2. below) vs a synthetic target (the Files target dependency) user's might not know what to make of when executing goals like `list`, `dependencies` or `filemap`.

The `default_sources_glob` makes less sense. You don't describe what that would be used for, but I can only see to cases and both have problems:
1. The `PythonRequirementLibrary` is declared in a BUILD directly where the BUILD has a sibling requirements.txt. Now you get a bogus source dependency on requirments.txt for that `PythonRequirementLibrary`.
2. If handed a source, whether via default globs or an explicit source argument, the `PythonRequirementLibrary` expands the source to requirements like the `python_requirements` macro currently does. This would lead to fat `PythonRequirementLibrary` targets that own all the requirements in a requirements.txt file which is almost always not what you want.
