`AppRunner` **is** a part of aiohttp 3.0 public API, not implementation detail.
The class appears as solution for people who don't want to call blocking `run_app` for running aiohttp server.
If proposed snippet works to you -- no need for new app signal.
Just a few extra lines instead of simple `run_app` call.

By running your snippet I see `404 Not Found` response but SSL certificate checks all passed.
aiohttp does nothing special with certs but uses built-in Python support.
In turn Python is not shipped with certificates bundle but uses OS one.

`requests` uses `certifi` for root certificates source. This approach has own advantages and disadvantages. Several years ago we decided to not rely on custom certificate libraries. 

But you can override aiohttp defaults by passing `ssl=ssl.SSLContext()` into `session.get()`.
It's up to you how to construct SSL context and what certificates to load into.
As second option you can update certificates on your MacOS X globally (sorry, I have no idea how to do it, I'm Ubuntu user)..

The second thing is intentional: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/commit/6c9a5200bf4c74962b8de20ccd9a34e719eac1ab

~~~
Give more information to the extensible methods.

Previously we were only giving the information that is needed by the
current implementation. But extensions might need more,
e.g. punishPredictedColumn might treat winner cells differently.
~~~

These methods exist for extensibility. The TM would be less extensible if, for example, an overridden `activatePredictedColumn` method couldn't use the matching segments. Basically, `activateCells` is calling these black-box methods. It gives the methods all of the possibly-relevant state because it doesn't know which subset of the state will be used (because the method is a black box).

The FEA language requires a series of "languagesystem" statements at the beginning of the file, which spell out all scripts and langsyses the font uses. Those are the langsyses which will be written into GSUB and GPOS if both types of features are used. 

It might be reasonable to ask the makeOTF and feaLib developers to always write both GSUB and GPOS, with these languagesystems listed in each, even if no features and lookups are written in one of the tables.

Actually, --mti-sources only works with masters. In fact the only thing the pipeline can interpolate right now is UFO. So the only way to interpolate GDEF rules is to define them via UFO anchors and kerning, then have FDK feature files generated on the fly, per-instance as part of compilation to OTF/TTF. And I'm not sure if the FDK feature file generation is sophisticated enough to handle these GDEF rules (no idea off the top of my head if any other scripts we're processing now use chaining contextual positioning).

So the options as I see them now are 1) generate master OTF/TTFs with FontChef sources and wait for binary interpolation or 2) look into adding this functionality to FDK feature source generation.

actually this looks like the default behavior of `SkPath::close()` method to always add a lineTo "verb" when the last lineTo is not already on the same point of the starting point.
You can see by calling `path.dump()` (which prints to standard output a text representation of the C++ object) after you have called closedPah on the pen (which simply forwards to the SkPath::close()).

```python
>>> path = Path()
>>> pen = path.getPen()
>>> pen.moveTo((100, 100))
>>> pen.lineTo((100, 200))
>>> pen.closePath()
>>> path.dump()
path.setFillType(SkPath::kWinding_FillType);
path.moveTo(100, 100);
path.lineTo(100, 200);
path.lineTo(100, 100);
path.close();
```

I think this is a non-issue, because both segment-wise and point-wise the two contour (with or without the last implied lineTo) are identical. To prevent that extra lineTo being called on the pen inside the Path.draw method, I would have to delay forwarding any incoming lineTo verbs from the SkPath::Iter::next until I'm sure that the following verb is _not_ a close verb.
Sure it could be done, but maybe at a later stage.

The problem in PGPy is that entire message content is stored into the memory using bytearray. This simply doesn’t work if the file size is bigger than you have memory. One possible solution is to use memory map:

https://docs.python.org/3/library/mmap.html

The needed changes are very minor:

```
diff --git a/pgpy/types.py b/pgpy/types.py
index b4a3d71..c13c65b 100644
--- a/pgpy/types.py
+++ b/pgpy/types.py
@@ -13,6 +13,7 @@ import os
 import re
 import warnings
 import weakref
+import mmap
 
 from enum import EnumMeta
 from enum import IntEnum
@@ -185,8 +186,8 @@ class Armorable(six.with_metaclass(abc.ABCMeta)):
     def from_file(cls, filename):
         with open(filename, 'rb') as file:
             obj = cls()
-            data = bytearray(os.path.getsize(filename))
-            file.readinto(data)
+            m = mmap.mmap(file.fileno(), 0, access=mmap.ACCESS_READ)
+            data = bytearray(m)
 
         po = obj.parse(data)
```


With this it’s possible to decrypt as big files as you want: 

```
[tornroos:~/elixir/pgp]$ ls -lh big_test_file.gpg 
-rw-r--r--  1 tornroos  staff    32G Sep 18 14:59 big_test_file.gpg
[tornroos:~/elixir/pgp]$ cat decrypt.py 
import pgpy

TEST_FILE = 'big_test_file.gpg'
PRIVATE_KEY = 'private.key'
PASSPHRASE = 'foobar'

key, _ = pgpy.PGPKey.from_file(PRIVATE_KEY)

with key.unlock(PASSPHRASE):
	message = pgpy.PGPMessage.from_file(TEST_FILE)
	decrypted_message = key.decrypt(message).message.decode("utf-8")
	print(decrypted_message) 

[tornroos:~/elixir/pgp]$ /usr/bin/time -l /usr/local/bin/python3 decrypt.py 
Command terminated abnormally.

  19225541  page reclaims

I started writing a client library to use the Blockstack API and I came across the following issues until know:

- The return values aren't documented. There are examples for all return values but no formal documentation that tells the datatype or which attributes are optional or required. It would be best to have the return structures documented like this:

Attribute | Datatype | Required
--- | --- | ---
passwd | String | Y
port | u16 | Y
regtest | bool | N
server | String | Y

- Inconsistent data types. Sometimes `true` is passed as a string `"True"` and sometimes it is passed as a boolean value. Same problem with number types. `"1234"` vs `1234`. There should be a consistent pattern. Either everything gets passed as a string or primitive types _never_ get passed as strings.

That's it for now. I'll post again if I stumble across more problems.

Here's a santized version of some notes I wrote up on the subject several weeks ago.

EntitySearchMixin
=================

I think it's about time to create an EntitySearchMixin class. This occurred to me while looking over some Jenkins failures recently. The jenkins failure involved some custom search code, and the process of troubleshooting the search code would be easier if a standard toolset for searching was available. I propose adding an EntitySearchMixin class which provides the following methods:

    search
    └── search_json
        └── search_raw
            └── search_payload

In the near term, the `search` method will not be implemented, as there are some significant barriers to implementing it. However, the other three `search_*` methods can be immediately created.

Search Structure
----------------

When a GET request is issued, a JSON payload must be provided. The payload has this basic structure:

    {
        'filter': '…',
        'filter2': '…',
        'search': 'key=… AND key2=…',
    }

The above is not as easy to implement as it appears at first glance, due to some annoying details. For example, here are some filters named by the API documentation:

* per_page (e.g. 20)
* order (e.g. 'name DESC')
* sort (e.g. {'name', 'DESC'})
* name (e.g. 'ACME Corp')
* organization_id (e.g. 5)

Notice that some of the filters, such as `per_page` and `sort`, are very generic and can apply to all search results, but other filters such as `name` and `organization_id` are specific to the type of entity being searched. Notice also that the `sort` and `order` filters are both very generic, but appear to conflict with each other.

The search string itself is also highly variable. Foreman API paths expect key-value pairs to be joined with the '=' character, whereas Katello API paths expect key-value pairs to be joined with the ':' character. Upstream will unify the syntax over time, but it is not the case right now. Also, search strings are arbitrarily complex. The AND and OR operators may be used, and I suspect that queries can be made more complex by adding parentheses. Also, search terms must be escaped so that characters like '=' do not interfere. The API documentation provides no guidance on how to perform escaping, and some questioning in IRC yielded "my guess is wrap it in double quotes".

Robottelo Search Implementation
-------------------------------

To handle all of the above, here is what I suggest doing:

### Mark Fields

Add a `search_filter=False` argument to `Field.__init__`'s parameter list. This will make it possible to mark individual fields as being filters in a very natural way. For example:

    class Product(…):
        organization = orm.OneToOneField('Organization', required=True, search_filter=True)
        name = orm.StringField(required=True, search_filter=True)
        label = orm.StringField()

### Method Responsibilities

The `search_payload` method should be responsible for generating a payload that can be used in an HTTP GET request. This method is responsible for reading data from the current entity (`vars(self)`) and transforming it in to something that Satellite understands. This means adding "_id" and "_ids" to the end of key names, placing query components either into the search string or using it as a search filter, placing either '=' or ':' in the search string, and so on. Depending on how complex this task is, it may be appropriate to split the search string generation logic into a separate method.

The `search_raw` method should be responsible for making the actual HTTP GET call. A call might look like this:

    Product(organization=5, name='Kool Aid').search_raw(auth=('Alice', 'hackme'))

It may be appropriate to add a facility to passing extra filters at this level. For example:

    Product(organization=5, name='Kool Aid').search_raw(
        filters={'per_page': 30, 'page': 2},
        auth=('Alice', 'hackme'),
    )

Interestingly, this would provide a method of conducting custom searches:

    Product().search_raw(
        filters={
            'per_page': 30,
            'page': 2,
            'organization_id': 5,
            'search': 'description="Purple Flavor!" OR description="Grape Flavor!"',
        },
        auth=('Alice', 'hackme'),
    )

This is an appealing approach. Search strings can be arbitrarily complex, and attempting to provide a layer of abstraction that generates all possible search strings is bound to fail (at the very least, it will be a very poor abstraction).

The `search_json` method should simply check for a status code that indicates an error and JSON-decode the response. It may also be useful to return just the 'results' section of the results instead of the full results. This is an open question.

The `search` method should return a tuple of entity objects that are pre-populated with IDs. No other attributes are pre-populated. Ideally, the full set of attributes for an entity should be lazily populated when an un-populated attribute is accessed, but that is a long-term goal.

Other Info
----------

A functioning `EntitySearchMixin` has been implemented. However, it has some subtle and nasty design issues. It should not be used, and the ideas described above should be implemented instead. Masochists can see the first implementation attempt here: https://github.com/Ichimonji10/robottelo/compare/search-mixin

> Or is there a workaround?

Probably could set up some branched workflow using the `FeatureUnion` meta-transformer, where the original categorical features is renamed in the beginning of both branches, so that you'd end up having `lookup(identitytransform(cat_feature))` and `lookup(cat_feature)`.

You can code up such "feature rename" transformation quite easily. The PMML representation of such transformation:
```XML
<PMML>
  <DataDictionary>
    <DataField name="x" dataType="string" optype="categorical"/>
  </DataDictionary>
  <TransformationDictionary>
    <!-- Same dataType and optype attribute values, but a different name attribute value-->
    <DerivedField name="alias(x)" dataType="string" optype="categorical">
      <FieldRef field="x"/>
    </DerivedField>
  </TransformationDictionary>
</PMML>
```

This patch reduces installation overhead when running `pre-commit run hookid` but can create strange output interleaving:

```console
$ pre-commit  clean && pre-commit  run flake8 --all-files
Cleaned /Users/asottile/.cache/pre-commit.
[INFO] Initializing environment for https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git.
[INFO] Initializing environment for https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit.git.
[INFO] Initializing environment for https://github.com/asottile/reorder_python_imports.git.
[INFO] Initializing environment for https://github.com/asottile/add-trailing-comma.
[INFO] Installing environment for https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git.
[INFO] Once installed this environment will be reused.
[INFO] This may take a few minutes...
Flake8...................................................................Passed
```

There's a few options for doing that today.
- Have a separate config and use `pre-commit run -c {alternate config filename}`
    - downsides: you'd potentially duplicate the common things
    - upsides: keeps things explicitly separate
    - you could even just put ~special things into a separate config and invoke both (once with the default config, once with the custom config)
- Configure specific hooks as [`stages: [manual]`](https://pre-commit.com/#confining-hooks-to-run-at-certain-stages) -- these won't run during the _normal_ workflow but you can trigger them by running `pre-commit run --hook-stage manual`
This has been released as part of [v1.7.0](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/releases/tag/v1.7.0)!

You could alternatively configure something like this:

```yaml
    -   id: mixed-line-endings
        args: [--fix=lf]
        exclude: '\.bat$'
    -   id: mixed-line-endings
        args: [--fix=crlf]
        files: '\.bat$'
```

pre-commit allows you to configure duplicate hooks / repositories already -- I don't think we should encode "special" cases based on file extensions (I personally dislike the choices made for the whitespace hook, heh).

Modifying by default is by-design.  Our goal is to have as little developer interaction as possible when committing.  Errors that require manual intervention increasingly encourage developers to `--no-verify` or `SKIP=...` which somewhat defeats the purpose of having the hooks at all.

I'd accept PRs to add options to turn off modification, but the default is going to be modifying.

> Hooks that modify the contents can be dangerous and surprising.
The hooks are very well tested and we use them on many and very large codebases without issue -- I wouldn't call the modification dangerous.

>  It would be more ergonomic to opt-in to each mutation behavior.
You're already opting into the hook by adding it to your config.

@nathanielford This is *not* related to the top issue. It is a long-standing Homebrew problem around Python formulae. Due to how they manage Python installations, every Python package formula would break every time you upgrade Homoebrew Python, until a new revision is released to correct the path. You should try `brew update` and `brew upgrade pipenv`, and if that doesn’t work, submit a bug report to Homebrew. There is nothing Pipenv can do here.

Side note: Personally I strongly discourage using Homebrew to install Python packages, or install any Python packages against the Homebrew Python using pip (or otherwise). It is just not a good idea.

> In addition to the proposed solution, I can imagine another solution -- what if attr: mypkg.version loaded the version statically [...]

On initial implementation I was thinking about a fallback for `ImporError` which ought to use pure parsing (something similar to https://github.com/idlesign/makeapp/blob/v0.12.1/makeapp/app_templates/__default__/setup.py#L19), but I resisted the temptation due to this parsing approach is too error prone for something that generic as `setuptools` (especially since we have a bunch of widely used approaches as where, how and what format to use to store version number).

> Any reason you can think the declarative config shouldn't support a file: for version number?

No, I think Barry's proposal it quite reasonable. Text files with just a single version line are quite common and can be easily generated by build tools. What bugs me about this reasoning is, that there is nothing preventing me from manually doing this. The problem with this however as outlined above is, that this breaks compatibility in case I move an endpoint to another peer. Having people to live with this due to some abstract reasoning just sounds unreasonable to me, especially if this is implemented as an **optional** feature.

Maybe I should provide some hard facts to support my case: A simple wamp call will result in 4 messages being exchanged overall. A wamp call to an endpoint which at the same time relies on 3 other endpoints (which is not an unusual number) results in **16** messages. That's **400% more workload for both CPU and traffic**.

Also I personally don't have any issues with having implementation specific features. Crossbar and other routers have implementation specific features as well. More importantly, we are not talking about `consequences beyond the component`. My POI on this is, that if all my endpoints are in the same session, I already have "priviliged access" to them, so we are more precisely talking about *consequences for the own component*. For me, such a feature is more of an *internal* implementation optimization than changing behaviour.

Not providing such a feature, given how relatively simple it is to implement and by which insane amount it increases performance sounds extremly unreasonable to me. Good luck trying to justify to any business guy, why we have to pay four times more money on hardware if a simple software change could fix that without consequences.

@DZabavchik yes, probably. I just made the timeout "huge" to ensure that I didn't timeout any connections because I wanted the "steady-state" period to have a lot of connections "doing pings". And, if I was ramping up "too fast" some connections would get nuked.

Just collecting more thoughts here, because I cannot completely oversee the extent of the problem yet: I wonder if it would make sense to move away from storing `Broker` instances on `Partition`. If we'd instead store only broker-ids and look up the real `Broker` instance only when accessed, we would automatically be more immune to failed brokers that may feature in replica-listings but aren't leaders on any of the partitions we care to access.

(This is also generally a safer setup, and would have avoided a bug @emmett9001 discovered the other week, where a `Partition` may hold an old, disconnected `Broker` instance while a new instance for the same broker-id was correctly available in `Cluster.brokers`).

I don't know if there is some expiration option based on waiting time. But I think there are 3 ways to deal with this. (Ideally combined together.)

1) There is an option "topology.message.timeout.secs" but it is the time from emitting tuple into topology to NACKing the tuple.

2) Use "topology.max.spout.pending" option. This option limits number of tuples emitted into topology. But you need to find optimal value for this option by yourself. So you don't have too much / too less tuples in topology

3) Use back-pressure mechanism. Which should limit number of tuples in topology based on some metrics (read more about this in [another article](http://jobs.one2team.com/apache-storms/) - sorry for external link but the topic is too vast)

We'd be happy to review PRs that fix bugs or add features, but PRs that fix these sorts of mechanical issues in source code aren't really needed.  They just create merge conflicts with outstanding patches.  The whitespace isn't perfect but it's not worth changing if that's going to conflict with other patches.  The maintainers can always make these sorts of changes or run a tool to do them.  The things we really want patches for are bugs.

So there's a couple things to note. I'll start with answering your question about the `GlobalObject` usage. `GlobalObject` is for serializing / deserializing references to globally-importable objects. This would be something you defined at module-level that can be imported - I imagine it could be a class, an instance or a function. For example

```
# mypackage.MY_OBJECTS
MY_OBJECTS = OrderedDict(...)
```

This `MY_OBJECTS` object is importable as `mypackage.MY_OBJECTS`. You can also perform a relative import if you use `GlobalObject(mypackage)`. Then deserialize would accept `.MY_OBJECTS` as well as `mypackage.MY_OBJECTS` and `mypackage:MY_OBJECTS`.

The `serialize` appears to be intended to always return an absolute import whereas the `package` argument controls what is accepted for deserialize.

> Also, what of the two naming schemes (zope_dotted versus pkg_resources)?

There are two similar specs for how to import an object from a string. Either a simple dotted string like `mypackage.MY_OBJECTS` or using the pkg_resources style which is a dotted string to the module and then colon (:) with the object inside the module after... such as `mypackage:MY_OBJECTS`.

At the end of the day I think `GlobalObject` has very limited use and you may not actually want to use it but that's your call.

> If I understand correctly, you are referring to using both __module__ and __name__ during serialization to compute the right string, correct?

Yeah the idea is to take an arbitrary class/function/object and figure out its importable path which is not particularly simple in Python so it requires a bit of guesswork. It's not possible afaik to figure out the importable path of an object... only the importable path of its class. For this reason I think `GlobalObject.serialize` itself probably has very limited usage.

The work-around I suggested to @mbruzek was to use a Docker container to test the new charm-tools, rather than a virtualenv.

I asked him to open this issue, though, so that we could discuss alternative ways of handling the `.pypi` dependencies.  Specifically, I was wondering why we didn't go with the approach of building a wheelhouse and then doing an install from that during the deploy?  It would make inspecting the built charm much cleaner without having to add filtering to the inspect library.  Also, a concern with the current approach is that we could still run afoul of issues with non-source-only pypi packages.  With the current scheme, dependencies are potentially being compiled on the build machine which could well be different than the deployed cloud image.  This doesn't come up often, but could be an issue, and I think the wheelhouse approach would resolve it.

So, I agree this should degrade a lot nicer and I'll put up a patch in a minute. However, could you better explain the circumstances you're running charm-tools with? - Because it takes time to land changes, but changes are needed immediately
- Because different people have different ideas about the API.
- Because we want to test updates on a canary project for a while before publishing it for all charms.
- Because there will be backwards incompatible changes.

The data for req should be string other than bytes, ie: you need to decode after base64 encode.
What's more, the encode method should be b64encode other than encodebytes.
The right code looks like:
```python
def tobase64(file_path):
    f=open(file_path,'rb')
    res=base64.b64encode(f.read())
    f.close()
    return res.decode()
```

We don't believe it is, it happens in cases where Chrome is not retrieving the CSS from the server, it is loading it from the cache. The exact set of steps we're seeing is something like this:

- Refresh page, icons load as expected
- Check Chrome Network tab, CSS was loaded as expected, viewing CSS in DevTools shows boxes in content: "" CSS
- Open DevTools then immediately refresh page
- Page loads, but icons are showing as odd characters (not boxes)
- Check Chrome Network tab, CSS is not mentioned (for this step we used Wireshark to watch what Chrome actually did, it did make a request to our server, our server responded with a 304 Not Modified)
- Check Source tab, CSS is present but the content: "" sections in the CSS have the same odd characters as the screen, even though the server did not retrieve the file from the server

At least in my case the RNASeq pipeline vary a lot on execution time (from a few hours to a few days) so running them one by one avoids possible combination of long running jobs. My feeling is that auto trunk is usable for cases with specified minimal job duration so that sos can automatically group small jobs ( with small specified wall time) to avoid submission of tiny jobs to cluster.

The `_vars` method is easy to explain because logically speaking the substeps can be considered to be executed by a for loop in the step namespace, so variables in the next substep naturally overwrite values from the previous substep (and this is exactly what has been done before `concurrent=True` and `task` were introduced). To this end we can even disable sharing variables from substeps for now  and provide `_vars` in a future release.

The slot files are per site. The default will use slot files because whatever parameter used, there might be other sos workflows downloading from the same site. There are potential problems with

1. One workflow has `max_jobs=10`, another has `max_jobs=2`, the it is likely that all slots will be occupied by the former workflow, leaving the latter wait until the first one is done (because 2 or more jobs will be running).
2. There can be more and more such slot files. No clean up right now.
3. Not quite sure brutal murder of sos process will do to subsequent runs. In the worst case a heavily-downloading workflow is killed, leaving a full slot file, and no one can download from the site until the slot file is cleared. I could potentially do something to recognize such residue files.

Rather than the magic construction of many estimators we might also just make a class that lets people wrap up a scikit-learn estimator and publish that?  They might be comfortable doing a straightforward step like this and it might reduce the amount of magic that we provide/maintain.

> But for a minibatch would we then multiply by the batch size? Or is it generally much cheaper to compute a batch than to compute many samples individually.

We can multiply by the batch size. The only ~~cheaper~~ faster way to calculate the gradient is to feed them through a GPU (which is why batch sizes are typically multiples of 32).

Calculating the gradient for many examples requires knowing the how wrong the prediction is on each example, and then adding up their gradients.

> training is just applying the model + back propagation (which is roughly the same order of magnitude)

Correct.

Details: the gradient calculation is done via [reverse mode autodiff], which typically requires no more than 5-6x more floating point operations for an typical gradient than a function evaluation. On my machine, it takes 2x longer on average to calculate the gradient vs getting the output with PyTorch's (simple) MNIST network.

(also, turns out autodiff is faster than hard-coding the gradient with NumPy even though it does more work because the graph autodiff requires can be parallelized: https://stsievert.com/blog/2017/09/07/pytorch/#speed)

[reverse mode autodiff]:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automatic_differentiation#Reverse_accumulation

Scope of uniqueness is within streaming session (across all devices). 
Streams with the same unique ID cannot co-exists (due to hardware limitations). 
This is how the Viewer knows to aggregate stream profiles into groups that can be turned-on together. 
While streaming, the unique ID is useful to address `frame_queues` or rendering buffers, or any other resource you have once-per-stream.

Please let me know if I should change anything or edit any of the documentation before I submit a pull request to trackpy.

Here are the lines I added to the code:
    # Calculation of biased weighted standard deviation
    numerator = ((msds.subtract(results))**2).mul(msds['N'], axis=0).sum(level=1)
    denominator = msds['N'].sum(level=1) # without Bessel's correction
    variance = numerator.div(denominator, axis=0)
    variance = variance[['<x>', '<y>', '<x^2>','<y^2>','msd']]
    std = np.sqrt(variance)
    std.columns = 'std_' + std.columns

               std_msd],

1. Load scene with seviri file that does not always have _all_ datasets available

3. Dataset is not loaded and a KeyError is *logged* but not raised all the way to the user (this is expected behavior)
4. Ask for a different dataset that _is_ present in the file

If having CONTRIBUTORS.txt and CONTRIBUTING.txt right next to each other is the concern, you could move CONTRIBUTORS.txt to somwhere in ``doc``.  When I contribute to a project I rarely need to look at a project's contributors, but I often need to know how to contribute.  This is particularly true when I am new to a project or when I am shifting between several projects in a day.

If you want to declutter the root directory, you could move the following files:
1. ``TASKS.txt`` could be included directly in ``doc/source/contribute.rst``.  The name ``TASKS`` doesn't seem to match the content and looking at it from the commandline doesn't seem to be very helpful for a new contributor.
2. ``TODO.txt`` could be moved to ``doc/release/``.  It looks like it would be more relevant to an experienced developer or release manager anyway, so it isn't clear it needs to have so much visibility.
3. ``DEPENDS.txt`` could be included directly in ``doc/source/install.rst``.  When I set up a development environment, I usually expect that the ``requirements.txt`` or ``pip install`` should handle dependencies for me.
4. ``RELEASE.txt`` could be moved to ``doc/release/``.  I suspect that only a few people will need to look at this file.  And those people should probably be familiar with where to find things in the repo.
5. Change ``requirements.docs.txt`` and ``requirements.optional.txt`` to ``requirements/docs.txt`` and ``requirements/optional.txt``.  I would expect to find requirements files in a directory named requirements, so I don't think this would add confusion.  You could also add a ``requirements/README.md`` explaining what the various requirement files contain, if you wish.
6. ``appveyor.yml`` could become ``.appveyor.yml`` so it doesn't show up with ``ls``.  This is the type of things I would expect to be hidden and shouldn't be necessary for new contributors to find from the commandline.
7. Remove ``bento.info`` and content in related files.  I think it is a dead project (correct me if I am wrong).  NumPy stopped supporting it a couple of years ago:  https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/6268

If any of that seems reasonable, I am happy to make a PR.

There isn't a wrapper for worktrees yet, but I found using Repo.git.worktree("add", folder, branch) will load the worktree for you.

From there you can navigate into the worktree and use it like you world any other worktree. 

To unload the worktree, simply remove the folder and then call Repo.git.worktree("prune").

This works with the latest version.

Full example:

import os
import git

os.mkdir(<desired-folder>)

repo = git.Repo()
print (repo) # You'll see the original branch path that you're on here

repo.git.worktree("add", <folder>, <branch>)

test_repo = git.Repo(<folder>)
print (test_repo) # Now you'll see that with this new git.Repo object that it's referencing your worktree
print (repo) # Just to confirm that this is pointing to the original location

# Run a simple command to make sure everythings working - might be better to use the wrapper library to confirm though
print (repo.git.status())
print (test_repo.git.status())

Sure, here's an example using `flask` and `requests-oauthlib`. I expect to see an error about [incorrect client credentials](https://developer.github.com/apps/managing-oauth-apps/troubleshooting-oauth-app-access-token-request-errors/#incorrect-client-credentials) because I deliberately add `invalid` to the `client_secret` value before using it:

```py
import flask
import os

from flask import request
from flask.json import jsonify
from requests_oauthlib import OAuth2Session

app = flask.Flask(__name__)

client_id = '<id from github>'
client_secret = '<secret from github>'

auth_url = 'https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize'
token_url = 'https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token'

@app.route('/')
def login():
    github = OAuth2Session(client_id)
    url, state = github.authorization_url(auth_url)
    return flask.redirect(url)

@app.route('/callback', methods=['GET'])
def callback():
    github = OAuth2Session(client_id)
    token = github.fetch_token(token_url, client_secret=client_secret+'invalid', authorization_response=request.url)
    return jsonify({'status': 'success'})

if __name__ == '__main__':
    os.environ['OAUTHLIB_INSECURE_TRANSPORT'] = "1"
    app.run(debug=True)
```

To reproduce the issue:

1. Register a new GitHub OAuth application with `http://localhost:5000/callback` as the callback URL
2. Put the client ID and client secret into the code above and run it
3. Visit `http://localhost:5000/` and complete the OAuth flow with GitHub

You should see the `MissingTokenError` instead of the actual response from GitHub. This makes debugging the issue pretty difficult, as it's not obvious that there's a typo in my client secret. I believe a similar thing will happen if the `redirect_uri` is incorrect, perhaps because one version uses HTTP while another uses HTTPS.

To add to this spec after discussions with @kalefranz, 
- installing in a private env should only work when the root env is active, otherwise, install in the current (or specified env) 
- there should be a `conda-meta/private_envs.json` in the root env (and only in the root) that keeps track of available private envs. It should have the form
```
<package_name>: <full path to the installed private env>
```

Sorry, I'm pretty sure that this is a conda issue.  You see it in conda-build because it uses conda, but the inherent issue is that conda is trying to move files that are in use.  That sometimes works on windows, and sometimes not.  The only true fix is to not move files that are in use.  We can either collect a batch or powershell script with the file operations to be performed after conda exits, or we can separate conda into its own install, so that when it runs, it is not changing files that conda itself is using.

- remove `export PATH=~/anaconda3/bin:$PATH` from your `.zshrc` file

Another optimization along these lines might be to reset the environment somehow instead of running deactivate scripts and then run activate scripts. There might be other tricks like this.

Taking a step back, historically we have discouraged activation scripts (particularly in c-f) and preferred options like patching hard coded paths for c-b to pick up. So there aren't lots of packages using activation (at least in c-f). So performance may be less of an issue than we think and the tricks mentioned above may already go a long ways.

>  I also mentioned somewhere else today that we're going to fairly soon run into issues with only having .sh and .bat scripts for activate and deactivate. We're having more and more users of csh variants, fish, and Powershell. Or people who want to use those shells at least. Not to mention xonsh, which in 4.4 already has a supported wrapper within conda.
>
> I think it's going to force us to work out a DSL or something similar that lets us manage environment variables in a more shell-agnostic way.

Interesting! Though this basically deserves it's own issue, right? Does it have one?

On Tue, Nov 8, 2016 at 3:14 PM, moonshoes87 <notifications@github.com>

> Ok, I found a hack-y solution.
>
> Instead of using setuptools "entry_points" (the preferred approach for
> making executables) I can use setuptools "scripts", which then allows me to
> write a custom setup script to launch one of the GUIs using pythonw. The
> downside with this approach is that I use this line to set the environment:
>
> /usr/bin/env pythonw
>
> which grabs whatever Python is in the $PATH. This is good in development
> but bad in production. Pip generally installs python scripts with the path
> to the Python you used to download that package hard-coded at the top, so:
> /Users/nebula/anaconda2/bin/python instead of /usr/bin/env python. What
> this means is that our installed script might be more prone to breaking if
> the user tries a different Python that isn't compatible. explained better
> by someone else here:
>
> #!/usr/bin/env python executes the system's default Python version. That
> is fine as long as your code is compatible with said version. When the
> default version is updated (from 2.5 to 3, say) your code or other Python
> code which references /usr/bin/env may stop working, even though the old
> Python version is still installed. For that reason it makes sense to
> "hardcode" the path to the appropriate python interpreter.
> http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1530702/dont-touch-my-shebang
>
> Anyway, what this means to me is that we should add Anaconda compatible
> scripts (that may be slightly less stable) to the existing pip package.
> From a UI perspective, what should we call those scripts? In testing I
> called the executables things like pmag_gui_a (a for anaconda), but maybe
> something else would be better? (Note that we can't use a command line
> argument here to specify anaconda vs non-anaconda -- it has to be the
> actual name of the executable). I would love input on this naming issue. I
> have pushed to master changes in command_line_setup.py and adding a bin/
> directory with the Anaconda scripts if anyone wants to have a look.
>
> You can also pip install a mostly-Anaconda compatible pmagpy-cli from the
> test PyPI site:
>
> pip install -i https://testpypi.python.org/pypi pmagpy-cli --upgrade
>
> That install should work with Anaconda with the commands pmag_gui_a,
> demag_gui_a, etc.
>
> Also, I've found several more incompatibilities with the Anaconda
> implementations of the GUIs. I'm going to keep working on those problems,
> and create new issues for any bugs that I need help with. Slow progress!

> The `BatchNormLayer` should be implemented similarly to the `BatchNormDNNLayer`, and then we could ditch the latter. We can also think about providing a way to use `std` instead of `inv_std` so we can leave all updates to cuDNN if the arguments allow.

I just tried the latter (it's actually `var`, not `std`), but curiously enough, for a simple example ([a WGAN on MNIST](https://gist.github.com/f0k/f3190ebba6c53887d598d03119ca2066)), training gets about 10% slower when leaving the updates to cuDNN instead of doing them ourselves. That's a pity, but the good news is we won't need to break the interface to store the running variance instead of the running inverse standard deviation. Unless I did something silly in my code, of course. My implementation is attached below.

<details>
<summary>An updated `BatchNormLayer` implementation that uses `T.nnet.bn`.</summary>
```python
class BatchNormLayer(lasagne.layers.BatchNormLayer):
    """
    Implementation of BatchNormLayer for recent Theano versions
    """
    def __init__(self, incoming, axes='auto', epsilon=1e-4, alpha=0.1,
                 beta=init.Constant(0), gamma=init.Constant(1),
                 mean=init.Constant(0), var=init.Constant(1), **kwargs):
        super(BatchNormLayer, self).__init__(
                incoming, axes, epsilon, alpha, beta, gamma, mean, var,
                **kwargs)
        self.var = self.inv_std
        del self.inv_std

    def get_output_for(self, input, deterministic=False,
                       batch_norm_use_averages=None,
                       batch_norm_update_averages=None, **kwargs):
        # Decide whether to use the stored averages or mini-batch statistics
        if batch_norm_use_averages is None:
            batch_norm_use_averages = deterministic
        use_averages = batch_norm_use_averages

        # Decide whether to update the stored averages
        if batch_norm_update_averages is None:
            batch_norm_update_averages = not deterministic
        update_averages = batch_norm_update_averages

        # Theano requires beta/gamma tensors; create dummies if needed
        shape = tuple(s for (d, s) in enumerate(input.shape)
                      if d not in self.axes)
        gamma = self.gamma or theano.tensor.ones(shape)
        beta = self.beta or theano.tensor.zeros(shape)

        # obtain self-normalized outputs and statistics, if needed
        if update_averages:
            (normalized, input_mean, input_inv_std, new_running_mean,
             new_running_var) = T.nnet.bn.batch_normalization_train(
                     input, gamma, beta, self.axes, self.epsilon,
                     self.alpha, self.mean, self.var)
        elif not use_averages:
            (normalized, input_mean,
             input_inv_std) = T.nnet.bn.batch_normalization_train(
                     input, gamma, beta, self.axes, self.epsilon)

        # normalize with stored averages, if needed
        if use_averages:
            normalized = T.nnet.bn.batch_normalization_test(
                    input, gamma, beta, self.mean, self.var, self.axes)

        # update stored averages, if needed
        if update_averages:
            # Trick: To update the stored statistics, we create memory-aliased
            # clones of the stored statistics:
            running_mean = theano.clone(self.mean, share_inputs=False)
            running_var = theano.clone(self.var, share_inputs=False)
            # set a default update for them:
            running_mean.default_update = new_running_mean
            running_var.default_update = new_running_var
            # and make sure they end up in the graph without participating in
            # the computation (this way their default_update will be collected
            # and applied, but the computation will be optimized away):
            dummy = 0 * (running_mean + running_var).sum()
            normalized = normalized + dummy

        return normalized
```
</details>

What you are getting is the scan download in 'nessus' format.  This is an export/import format for transferring scan results between Tenable products.  If you go towards the end of the file, you will see the ReportItems in the XML that have the results.

 - 'html' - HTML based report of the results.
 - 'csv' - Comma separated file with the results
 - 'db' - Database format that includes more information for transferring results.

I believe there may also be a 'pdf' format, but would have to confirm by looking at the api documentation on scan/download.

>
> I don't think this is quite right. The pixel shape depends on the total
> charge stored in the pixel and is independent of where in the pixel the
> photons landed. Arun's suggestion is a good one for deciding whether a
> photon stays in the undistorted pixel or moves to a neighboring pixel. If
> it lands near the center, then it is unlikely to move to a neighboring
> pixel, and there might be a way to use this information to speed up the
> decision of whether to move the photon or not. But I don't think it impacts
> the decision of when to update the pixel shapes. Hope this makes sense
>
I was not suggesting that the update itself depends on where the photons
land - I was thinking that when we update the pixel boundaries can depend
on the location of the photon. For each incoming photon, if it falls in the
central region of a pixel, then irrespective of the boundary distortions,
we can be confident that the photon is not going to contribute to the
electron count in any other pixel. We do not have to update the pixel shape
after we receive this photon. We can keep track of the number of photons we
have received since our previous update. Once a photon falls dangerously
close to the pixel boundary, say 10% of pixel size away from the edges of
the undistorted square pixels (which happens with probability of  ~20% and
hence 5x speed up?), then we update the pixel shape boundary using the
photon counts we have and then decide which pixel the latest photon falls
into. I think this is valid way to reduce the number of calls to new
subroutines.

Ah, I had forgotten the fact that they get downloaded into a subdirectory.  Good point, thanks.

> I guess the new catalog will include everything in the 23.5 catalog plus more, right?

I omitted an important detail.  The new catalog is a *fair subset* of all galaxies brighter than 25.2.  In practice, when we were doing the parametric fits for this fainter sample, it was pretty expensive to do everything in COSMOS, so we took a subset.  For that reason, if you were to take the new catalog and use only the <23.5 galaxies, you'd get something quite a bit smaller than the current catalog.  (New catalog has ~88k galaxies with I<25.2, while the current catalog has 56k with I<23.5.)

> But there are still some objects with half-light radii of only a few milliarcseconds. 

Yes, that's true.  If you use ```numpy.percentile``` on ```hlr_s``` you will see that the 1st percentile of the Sersic half-light radii is 0.0369 arcsec, so a bit under 1% of the objects have a half-light radius that is below 1 pixel.  My take on this is that there are going to be a rare subset of objects with very small half-light radii, and in general the uncertainty on their hlr values is high (i.e., for a fixed detection S/N, the uncertainty on hlr rises as the hlr shrinks because there isn't much information on it).  So I don't believe that their hlr are actually in the milliarcsecond range for real, and I think that if we had quoted uncertainties on the hlr, the uncertainties would reflect the fact that we shouldn't take these tiny numbers at face value.  And when we draw those galaxies as they would appear in a ground-based survey, that uncertainty shouldn't matter -- whether we got the "right" hlr of 0.04 arcsec or whatever it really is, vs. these wrong values of 0.003 arcsec, either way it should look basically like a point source.

So basically, yes, I don't believe those fit values in detail, but I don't think it matters too much either.

BTW part of my thinking here is that people who care in great detail what the galaxies will look like won't want Sersic fits, and will use the images themselves.  There are many other things wrong with using Sersic profiles, besides these tiny hlr values for 1% of the objects.

It isn't entirely clear what you are proposing overall, so I'll go over individual statements below.

By the way, Spinel isn't acronym, so you can just call it "Spinel", not "SPINEL".

> The simple approach would be to think the SPI-CS signal is the solution. The reason this fails is many SPI hosts will drop the CS to active, and immediately (within ZERO micro seconds) begin transmitting (clocking) data out of the SPI port.

This isn't actually a problem with [the recommended Spinel SPI protocol](https://rawgit.com/openthread/openthread/master/doc/draft-spinel-protocol.html#rfc.appendix.A.2). If the slave is just waking up and isn't ready to transmit, the slave would keep MISO high. This signals to the master that the slave is throttling. The master will then either poll (very dozen milliseconds or so) and/or wait for the interrupt pin to be asserted.

Granted, this could likely be better documented.

> The result is the Host & NCP slave can be "off-by-a-bit" or two, or three during the transfer and this problem only occurs rarely... and nobody can figure out why or when this occurs.. maybe the chip wakes up slower when it is very cold (think sensor inside a freezer, or a pool sensor in midwinter)
> What is missing is a "NCP-IS-READY" hand shake back to the host. (total 2 pins)

I emphatically disagree with any recommendation that requires more pins: It is simply not necessary.

If you and the host are "off by a bit or two", then the problem is in your implementation. If the NCP wakes up from CS being asserted and it can't guarantee that it can handle the transaction then it should keep MISO high and ignore the frame and wait for the next one. The master will then prepare another frame that will start at the next slave-to-master interrupt or wait a dozen (or so) milliseconds and try again (whichever is sooner). No harm, no foul.

> The NCP may need to wake the host up and tell the host there is data to be sent.
>
> This slightly discussed in the SPINEL protocol and repeated here for completeness.
>
> What is not considered is this: A battery powered Linux host might be asleep and in a low power state (example: A cell-phone-data-only device connected to an NCP) the NCP cannot just "raise the request" and start transmitting out the UART... that host might also need to "wake up" and turn clocks on, enable PLLs and so forth before the UART baudrate clock is operational.
>
> Typically another GPIO pair (ncp to HOST) is used to indicate this request.
> total: 4 pins.

Traditionally, we have done this using flow control and BREAK signals. When the host goes to sleep, it asserts flow control. When the NCP has something to send to the master while flow control is asserted, it will occasionally send a UART "break" until flow control is no longer asserted, at which point frames are transmitted as normal.

The same mechanism can be used with software flow control (requiring only two pins), as long as the underlying hardware treats BREAK as a specific UART signal, but this may be rather tricky to get right.

Another way that this could be handled is with a serial reliability layer. This is nice because flow control is implicit and wouldn't require additional pins (at the cost of some additional wakeup latency). But this isn't yet specified.

I agree this needs to be better elaborated on in the appendix. We also haven't implemented this yet in the OpenThread NCP, so we need to go ahead and do that.

> The economic need is this:
>2 pins UART (txd/rxd) + 2 GPIO handshake = total 4
>3 pins SPI (mosi,miso,clock) + 2 GPIO handshake = total 5

We use TXD, RXD, CTS, RTS for UART, using the signaling scheme I described above. Works great. We could define a mechanism which only uses TXD and RXD, but for that we would need a reliability and retransmission layer which hasn't been specified.

For SPI we use MOSI, MISO, CLK, CS, and INT. We also use RESET. Theoretically RESET isn't required, but we have found that it is useful for the master to have the ability to force a hardware reset of the slave out-of-band. Also, theoretically even INT isn't required, since you can always poll with the current SPI protocol, further reducing pin count.

> Notice that the SPI does not use an actual CS signal

It seems like you are proposing that we change the SPI protocol to require a two-way handshake before the master starts clocking out bits, something like Master-Ready and Slave-Ready, where if at any point one side wants to start a transaction, that side asserts its pin and then waits for the other side to assert their pin. The signal of asserting CS is then effectively the logical AND of the state of both pins (at least for the starting edge), where de-asserting CS is (presumably) just the falling edge of Master-Ready.

I don't like this system for the following reasons:

* It doesn't solve any problem that isn't already sufficiently solved by the current recommended protocol
* It makes the implementation that drives the master from Linux user-space more complex.
* It prevents (or reduces the efficiency of) sharing the SPI bus with other peripherals.

We are using the Spinel-recommended SPI protocol right now and it works great.

That being said, I think the documentation could use improvement, which we are working in internally.

If the user omits an unknown token from `specials`, they're asserting that no unseen tokens will exist in the dataset. We should probably check this and throw an error when we try to index `stoi` with such a token, but other than lacking that error the behavior reported is essentially intentional. In particular, it's important that removing an unknown token from `specials` shifts the other tokens into the zero position, because the motivation for adding the option of no unknown token was to avoid having an unnecessary extra option in the softmax.

        (REPLY_USER, 'Ответ от пользователя'),
        (REPLY_ADMIN, 'Ответ от админа'),
        (NOTE, 'Заметка'),
        (TAG_ADDED, 'Добавлен тег'),  # В body лежит тег
        (TAG_DELETED, 'Удален тег'),  # В body лежит тег

        # Когда пользователь закрывает диалог - у него появляется CLOSED.

        (CHANNEL_CHANGED, 'Изменен канал диалога'),
        (CHANNEL_RESTORED, 'Восстановлен канал диалога'),
    )

    USER2APP = 'u2a'
    APP2USER = 'a2u'
    DIRECTIONS = ((USER2APP, 'User -> app'), (APP2USER, 'App -> user'))

# Choice items are generated automatically, based on Django models constants
CHConversationPartDirection = IntEnum('Enum', [c[0] for c in ConversationPart.DIRECTIONS])
CHConversationPartType = IntEnum('Enum', [c[0] for c in ConversationPart.TYPE_CHOICES])

# ClickHouseBaseModel inherits models.Model from clickhouse-orm and doesn't contain any field attributes
class ClickHouseCollapseModel(ClickHouseBaseModel):
    """
    Adds operations for  engine CollapsingMergeTree
    https://clickhouse.yandex/reference_en.html#CollapsingMergeTree
    """
    # ClickHouse field name, containing sign for collapse operations
    clickhouse_sign_field = "sign"

    # Number of last inserted row. It is added automatically to each row on insert or update
    # It is used to get the last version of data, if it was not collapsed yet.
    _version = fields.UInt32Field(default=0)


class CHConversationPart(ClickHouseCollapseModel):
    id = fields.UInt64Field()
    created = fields.DateTimeField()
    created_date = fields.DateField(materialized='toDate(created)')

    first = fields.UInt8Field(default=0)
    conversation_id = fields.UInt64Field()

    part_group_id = fields.UInt64Field()

    inbound_email_id = fields.UInt64Field(default=0)
    body = fields.StringField()
    user_id = fields.UInt64Field(default=0)
    django_user_id = fields.UInt64Field(default=0)
    app_id = fields.UInt64Field()

    direction = fields.Enum8Field(CHConversationPartDirection)
    email_message_id = fields.StringField()
    sent_via = fields.StringField()
    type = fields.Enum8Field(CHConversationPartType)

    read = fields.UInt8Field(default=0)

    sign = fields.Int8Field(default=1)

    engine = engines.CollapsingMergeTree('created_date', ('id', 'app_id'), 'sign')

    # Not field service attributes
    django_model = ConversationPart
    auto_sync = 5

As further investigation shows, the issue is in clickhouse-cityhash module, please take a look at explanation below:

Part of the pip install output

clickhouse-cityhash
```
  running install_lib
    copying build/lib.linux-x86_64-2.7/clickhouse_driver/util/cityhash.so -> /usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/util
```

clickhouse-driver
```
 writing manifest file 'clickhouse_driver.egg-info/SOURCES.txt'
    removing '/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver-0.0.6-py2.7.egg-info' (and everything under it)
    Copying clickhouse_driver.egg-info to /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver-0.0.6-py2.7.egg-info
    running install_scripts
```

If clickhouse-driver has been installed with any type of compression support it install as a dependency clichouse-cityhash package. 
Under CentOS clickhouse-cityhash package goes to **/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver** and actual clichouse-driver goes to **/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver**.

In CentOS by default in PYTHONPATH variable: /usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages goes first and then /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages

Since both paths containing clickhouse_driver but in **/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver** obviously no client.py - import fails.

**Workaround:**
move /usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/util/cityhash.so to /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/util/

remove /usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver

**Possible solution:**
Install clickhouse-cityhash package to clickhouse_cityhash/ directory instead of clickhouse_driver/
(Some changes in driver code/imports may be required)

How do you emit insert query? It seems that you are trying to do something like:
```python
client.execute("INSERT INTO test (x) VALUES (1), (2)")
```

But the client doesn't parse query like `clickhouse-client`. The proper way of emitting INSERT query:
```python
client.execute(
    'INSERT INTO test (x) VALUES',
    [{'x': 1}, {'x': 2}, {'x': 3}, {'x': 100}]
)
client.execute('INSERT INTO test (x) VALUES', [[200]])
```

See examples of usage: https://github.com/mymarilyn/clickhouse-driver.

I have a related concern, but I'll state it a little differently.

I did not understand the docs at first glance, and had to read https://github.com/click-contrib/click-configfile/blob/master/click_configfile.py#L468-L499 to figure out what's going on. The first section listed when you construct your own `class ConfigSectionSchema` is the "Primary", which means that it will have it's keys / values loaded into the top level of the `ctx.default_map` dict. This has the effect that a key in this section has the following precedence: CLI > Configuration file > Environment > Default.

That's great, and the order I want. The problem for me is controlling the other sections in the config file. I really want that precedence of CLI > Configuration file > Environment > Default, but I'm finding it hacky / difficult to achieve. Every other section (not the primary) is loaded as a dict into the `ctx.default_map`, so it would look like `{'non-primary-section': {'key': 'val1'}, 'key-from-primary-section': 'val2'}`.

This effectively removes the config-provided key from the precedence entirely, to give you just CLI > Environment > Default, plus the value from the config available inside `ctx.default_map['non-primary-section']` to process manually. While you have the data from the config, how should I determine whether or not to use it? I can't easily tell if I got the original ctx var as part of the cli, environment var, or default value, so I don't know if I should override it or not with the value from the config file.

### Possible Improvements / Solutions:

1) I understand the desire to keep the namespaces clean from the different sections, so perhaps the default behavior should stay. However, I would love the ability to denote whether a SectionSchema should assume the behavior of the primary schema manually. If I could, then I could choose to merge as many schemas into `ctx.default_map` as I want. I imagine for many projects this would not be an issue. I see this as an additional feature in this project.

2) A clean way of determining the source of the params on a command. If we can find out if it's from one source or the other, we can force the same precedence. I don't think click exposes this right now. It could, potentially though, and just be another kind of meta data.

Maintaining session state across multiple events is a tricky problem because we don't want to assume any specific ordering of events. An event missing right now may arrive a bit later because of network issues.

One possible way of determining the correctness of a session (without relying on the order of events) could be done with:

```
- Name: game_start_and_end
  Type: list

Currently with Ray's API, there is no way to delete objects. If you simply stop using an object, then it will be evicted at some point.

As a quick hack, if you want to protect an object, you can regularly access it (e.g., by calling `ray.get` on the object). The eviction proceeds by evicting the least recently used objects first, so if you recently accessed an object, it will be less likely to be evicted. However, this is more of a short-term hack than a real solution.

One limitation is the fact that the object store is currently entirely in memory and so the number and sizes of the objects you have are limited by the amount of memory you have.

The use case is to potentially handle sets of object IDs which are larger than memory all together. For example, we would like to represent a dataset of videos like Python array of `[objectIds]`, where objectId is a decoded video into a 3D numpy array (2D frame, 1D time). And then we would like to send this dataset around. And some other functions would then go an iterate one by one on each sample (video) and do something with it. Now, if we in advance decode all videos, we will probably run out of memory and objects will start getting discarded. And when then task starts reading one by one, it will have to recreate them anyway. So putting a lazy flag could help us to hint Ray that this will happen. You can also imagine that we do a map on that list, where every worker gets a subset of videos to process. It is better that video gets decoded on demand on that same worker, than that they get decoded on one worker which happened to create the Python list of all videos, and then send over to workers who will deal with videos. We could do probably ourselves by creating subsets of videos first, but we would like to have separate dataset building and then processing of datasets. So building would create this lazy object, and then only when consumers start using it, on a worker to consume it, they will decode the video.

The workaround for `__init__` is what you mention. The workaround for other cases (which would be generally in the context of `@staticmethod`, since that's where you explicitly need the class name) is to just use `@classmethod` instead `@staticmethod` so that you can say `cls.foo()` instead of `MyClass.foo()`. @rodolfolottin Assuming that you want to keep the distribution constant, and for a simple network like: `A ---> B`. And let's say you changed `P(A)` to `P'(A)`, you can simply use this: `P'(B|A) = P(A) * P(B|A) / P'(A)` where `P'(B|A)` is the new CPD for `B`. And pgmpy already has methods for doing product and division on `DiscreteFactor` (http://pgmpy.org/factors.html#discrete-factor), so you can directly use that. But since we don't have product and divide methods for `TabularCPD`, you will need to convert the `TabularCPD` objects to `DiscreteFactor` (http://pgmpy.org/factors.html#pgmpy.factors.discrete.CPD.TabularCPD.to_factor) and do the operations and convert it back. 

The code should roughly look something like this (I haven't tested it):
```python
A_factor = cpd_a.to_factor()
B_factor = cpd_b.to_factor()
A_new_factor = cpd_a_new.to_factor()
B_new_factor = A_factor * B_factor / A_new_factor
cpd_b_new  = cpd_b.copy()
cpd_b_new.values = B_new_factor.values
```

semantics and word perspectives.. :)
`policy` implies something formal, which may have a perception of 'management' (set in stone) rather than 'maintenance' (can be changed).
`action` implies something dynamic; your choice will make something happen.
`method` implies something more generic across contexts.
`rule` implies something less generic that could be confusing across contexts.

Sorry for not keeping being satisfied with this. But here's an idea that maybe could make implementation and API simpler. What about of instead of doing:

```
param = Param(batch_mode=MIN)
run(self): print(self.param)  # prints 3

or

param = Param(batch_mode=MAX)
run(self): print(self.param)  # prints 9

or

param = Param(batch_mode=LIST)
run(self): print(self.param) # prints [3,5,9]
```

I just thought if we could do something like this:

```
param = Param(batch=True)
run(self): print(min(self.param))  # prints 3

or

param = Param(batch=True)
run(self): print(max(self.param))  # prints 9

or

param = Param(batch=True)
run(self): print(self.param) # prints [3,5,9]
```

Basically, I'm seeking out to know if we really need the server to care about if it's min/max/list. And let all aggregation be simple user-defined logic using already existing python stuff.

First thing of course, is the Decimal class is slow compared to raw integers or floats. This is python after all, and this seems to be called in a critical section (or at least as shown below -- multiple times per message).

Looking at my profiling data, it shows that `BackoffTimer.success` is called 50001 times (which is equal to the number of messages received), and `BackoffTimer.get_interval` is called 100,040 times.

* `success` seems to be called by `on_backoff_resume`, which is called by trigger which is called by lots of people.. 
* `get_interval` seems to be called
  * 50001 times by `maybe_update_rdy`, called by `_handle_message`
  * 50001 times by `enter_continue_or_exit_backoff`, called by `_on_backoff_resume`

If that's unexpected, perhaps the backoff code needs some restructuring and we'd see even more performance boost. :)

Okay, so regarding the business of "tips", specifically a TDD tip and what I'll call the "spike" tip.

The only thing that matters for getting your PR merged is the TDD tip. This is essentially the sequence of commits that are ready to be merged. It might not be a complete feature yet, but what is there is good enough to merge into the main python-pptx branch.

In addition to these commits, it's not uncommon to have additional commits where you are experimenting to see how things should be implemented etc. Because this work is unsupported by tests, it is called a *spike*. The work in these commits is undertaken for the sake of learning, but it's not uncommon for important parts of it to be incorporated in later commits on the TDD branch.

One consequence of working this way is that you need two branches. Let's call them "tdd" and "spike" for the sake of this conversation, you can call them whatever suits you.

Right now, the `tdd` branch would point at the `docs:..` commit and the `spike` branch would point to the the very end of this branch, I expect the same commit as your `feature/bookmarks` is pointing to now.

The basic mechanism is that you add new commits on your `tdd` branch, then rebase the `spike` branch onto each new commit. Over the series of commit/rebase steps, the contents of the spike branch get "consumed" as they are incorporated in TDD commits, until the spike disappears completely. This process is known as "continuous spiking". I first learned it from Gary Bernhardt. https://www.destroyallsoftware.com/screencasts/catalog/spiking-and-continuous-spiking

For our purposes here, all I care about as the committer is a sequence of mergeable commits that in the end constitute a feature. The spike has no inherent value for me.

For the developer (you) though, spiking can be very useful as a way to work out how to actually implement something, ask for feedback, etc. So it's a great idea I think in this case.

The continuous spiking process demands full understanding of rebasing, which is why we visited that early on.

So basically, I'm proposing that if you're ready to get started with the TDD pass (even if you want to do more spiking later), that the right next step is to get your TDD branch/tip going by establishing the first commit on that branch, the `docs:...` commit. You already have one of course, you just need to incorporate the changes you've made in later commits into that first commit. The "checkout file from spike" procedure I mentioned in my prior post is the quickest way to do that in my experience.

My original report sort of buried the feature request, so let me try to state it more clearly:

  * **The LE client should allow writing dummy, self-signed certs / keys.**
  * Details:
    * It should use all the usual args (e.g., `--domain` and `--config`) to determine where to write these files.
    * It should NOT overwrite valid certs that already exist at these file locations.
    * It should NOT expect a webserver to be running.
    * It must write correctly formatted certs / keys (e.g., not just `touch` the files), otherwise nginx will refuse to load.

And the purpose of this mode is to support automated deployment, so that deploy rules can:
  1. Run the LE client to write the dummy certs (if none exist).
  1. Place a deploy-managed nginx config file that references these certs.
  1. Ensure nginx is running.
  1. Run the LE client to generate real certs, overwriting the dummy certs.

I think the main use case for it would be automation, in situations where you've already confirmed via an interactive run that the "all domains" default is right for you. It allows you to make a "greedy" certificate, that adds new domains as they're added to your server config.

> For calling it from run, it would be specifically for the case where we're reinstalling an existing cert. I suppose that could be called after calling certonly.

I don't think this works at least with the current form of "reinstall" for `certonly`/`run`. All that's saying is we don't need to obtain a new certificate, you just need to install it. You could run `certbot certonly --standalone` and then later run again with `run` and Apache or Nginx and this would be considered a "reinstall" if your cert hasn't expired.

I think for both our use case and the one that motivated this PR, `deploy` and `renew_deploy` are likely to basically be identical. For us with Nginx, if we see comment "managed by Certbot", you know these lines were created by us on a previous run and we can change them to satisfy the request. For the use case that motivated this PR, maybe there is less work to be done with `renew_deploy`, but that's purely speculation and both have to upload the new certificate and configure the remote webserver to use it.

If the comments we add in Nginx aren't sufficient for some reason, we could get around this by changing our behavior based on whether or not the `installer` from the renewal config is the same, but this seems more complex than it has to be. We added the "managed by Certbot" comments specifically so we can differentiate between values set by us and those set by the user.

The issue is introduced when we moved `kolibri/dist` to the front of PYTHONPATH. In this case, Kolibri doesn't work with Python 3 on OS X nor Ubuntu 14.04, showing the error described above (haven't tried other versions of Ubuntu yet, but I assume they would have the similar result). This error is because the [`future` package](https://github.com/PythonCharmers/python-future/blob/v0.16.0/setup.py#L63) (from `morango`) depends on the `html` module for python 2, which installs `html` in `kolibri/dist`. However, this `html` module shouldn't be used for Python 3, as Python 3 is supposed to use its default `html` module. Having `kolibri/dist` in the front of PYTHONPATH makes kolibri import html from `kolibri/dist` instead of Python 3's own packages, which caused the error.

I tried to move the `kolibri/dist` to the end of PYTHONPATH, as we did before. In this case, it works on OS X but not on Ubuntu 14.04. This is because Ubuntu 14.04 has its system library `requests` with version 2.2.1, which is older than the one we have in `kolibri/dist`. This system `requests` module doesn't have `packages.py`, which is used in `utils/Transfer.py` to download channels. Therefore, when we move the `kolibri/dist` to the end of PYTHONPATH, it will cause an error when we trying to import channels from Studio.

Buttons are disabled to prevent the user from going forward and downloading a second content metadata DB concurrently. They get freed up once the database is downloaded and the user is free to go to another channel.

The basic test is that the endpoint loads, which we have for all endpoints. We don't have a UI test framework, so that shouldn't be a requirement -- though we do need someone to take ownership of that need in the future. Regarding the "hide from normal users", the entire app is flask-admin. My understanding is that we can enable authentication, but not authorization, because everything is behind the admin URI. So, you should be able to add the @login_required decorator and test with an authentication backend (e.g. ldap). I haven't set that up since Airbnb and Agari both authenticate at an apache reverse proxy, not in Airflow. 

Try sending an email to the dev list to help test your feature for this use case and also ask for help is setting up an auth backend to ensure only authenticated users can see it. 

For any contributor to test, they just need to run `dev/airflowpr work_local`. This will set up your PR to test and it's a snap.
